review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Orientation session today. Sat in the back row out of habit, then realized I was the only one over forty in the entire lecture hall. The program coordinator kept talking about "building your professional foundation" and I wanted to raise my hand and say that some of us already had a foundation, thank you, before it was demolished without warning.

I kept my mouth shut. That is what you do. You do not embarrass yourself by complaining about circumstances nobody asked about. But walking back to my apartment afterward, I felt it — that heaviness in my chest. I am not sure if this was the right decision. Forty thousand dollars a year to be lectured about organizational behavior by someone who has never actually run an organization.

Nudge: "What would make this decision feel worth it by the time you graduate?"

Response: A title that commands respect again. A role where people come to me for guidance, not the other way around. I suppose I want to walk into a room and have people assume I belong there, the way they used to.

Group project assigned today. I was placed with three twenty-somethings who immediately started a WhatsApp thread I had to ask them to explain how to join. They scheduled our first meeting for 10 PM on a weeknight.

Could not sleep after writing that last note. The 10 PM meeting is bothering me more than it should. Back when I managed the retail lending division, if someone scheduled a meeting for 10 PM, there had better have been an emergency — a systems failure, a regulatory audit, something serious. These kids treat it like nothing.

I know I am supposed to adapt. That is the whole point of coming back to school — proving I can learn new ways of working. But there is a difference between adapting and abandoning every standard you spent a career developing. I ran teams. I built processes. I had an executive assistant who managed my calendar. Now I am asking a twenty-three-year-old named "Brayden" how to download an app on my phone.

The worst part is that I smiled and thanked him. What else was I supposed to do? Throw a tantrum about how things used to be? I have seen those people. They become cautionary tales whispered about in break rooms. So I will show up to the 10 PM meeting and I will contribute my ideas and I will be pleasant about all of it. That is the only path forward.

But I am writing this at 11:47 PM because I cannot stop replaying the moment when Brayden said "it's super easy, here let me show you" with that patient voice you use for elderly relatives who cannot work the remote control.

Nudge: "You mentioned you smiled and thanked him. What would have happened if you'd responded differently — said something honest about how that moment felt?"

Professor Chen called on me in class today and I gave a detailed answer drawing on my experience restructuring our branch network in 2019. She nodded politely and said "that's one perspective, but let's look at what the research shows." Then proceeded to present a case study that contradicted half of what I said.

I stayed after class to discuss it further and she said my "practitioner insights are valuable" but that academic rigor requires us to "question our assumptions." I am not sure what assumptions she thinks I have. I was there. I saw what worked. The branch network I restructured is still operational — or it was until the whole bank got acquired.

My daughter texted me later asking how school was going. I told her it was fine.

Nudge: "When you told your daughter things were fine, was that to protect her from worrying, or something else?"

Response: She has her own problems to deal with. My job is to handle mine.

The 10 PM meeting went until midnight. Brayden talked for forty-five minutes about "leveraging synergies" without saying anything concrete. I drafted the entire project outline afterward because someone had to. Submitted it to the group chat at 2 AM.

Interesting development. The group used my outline almost word for word. Brayden presented it in class as "our collaborative framework" and the professor praised the "clear structure and practical grounding." Nobody mentioned that I wrote it. I did not correct them.

Here is the thing though. I felt something shift when I saw the professor nodding along. That familiar feeling from the old days — when you put something together that works and people respond to it. The recognition does not need to be public. I know what I contributed. The professor knows the quality improved dramatically between our brainstorm and our deliverable.

Maybe this is how it works now. You do the work, you let other people take the stage, you build influence through competence rather than title. I am not sure I like it. But I am starting to see that there might be a way to operate here that is not completely foreign to who I am.

My ex-wife used to say I cared too much about getting credit. She meant it as criticism. Maybe she had a point, or maybe she did not understand what it is like to have your competence be the only thing you can rely on. Either way, the work got done. That matters.

Nudge: "What made you decide not to correct them about the outline? Was it strategic, or did something else hold you back?"

Response: I suppose I was testing something. Whether I could let go of the credit and still feel like myself. And I did, mostly. But there was also this — if I had spoken up, I would have been the old man demanding acknowledgment. I have seen how quickly people dismiss that. Better to let the work speak and see who notices.

Career services appointment today. The counselor — probably thirty years old at most — asked me what kind of "pivot" I was hoping to make. I explained that I am not pivoting, I am rebuilding. She looked confused.

I walked her through my background and she said the market is "challenging" for candidates my age but that I have "transferable skills." Transferable. As if thirty years of leadership is a foreign currency that needs to be exchanged for something more useful. I thanked her for her time and left with a folder of job search resources I will probably never open.

Midterm grades posted. I received the highest score in the section on my case analysis. Professor Chen left a comment: "Exceptional integration of theory and practice." I printed it out and put it in my desk drawer.

Nudge: "Why did you print it? What will having that piece of paper there do for you?"

Response: Evidence. Proof that I was not imagining things — that I do know what I am doing, even in this environment. On the days when I feel like an artifact, I can open that drawer and see that someone with credentials agrees I have something to offer.

Group presentation today. I suggested we rehearse beforehand but was outvoted. "We'll figure it out live," Brayden said. So we went in unrehearsed and it showed — stumbling over transitions, contradicting each other on key points. Professor Chen gave us a B-minus and commented that our "individual insights were strong but integration was lacking."

I wanted to say something afterward but held back. What would be the point? They would just see me as the cranky old guy who cannot roll with informality. Better to take the grade and move on. But it grates. I built my reputation on preparation. On never walking into a room without knowing exactly what I was going to say and why. Watching twenty years of professional instincts get overridden by committee feels like swallowing glass.

Sunday afternoon. First time in weeks I do not have something due tomorrow. Went to a coffee shop near campus and just sat for a while. Watched the students come and go with their laptops and their confidence and their entire futures stretching out in front of them.

I remember that feeling. Not the specifics — I was not a coffee shop person at twenty-five — but the sense that time was on my side. That I could make mistakes and recover. That every door led to another door. Now I feel like I am running out of doors. Or maybe I already picked one and the rest all closed behind me.

Here is what I have been circling around for weeks: I came back to school because I could not stand being unemployed. Being introduced at parties as "Harold, he's figuring out his next chapter." The pity. The sudden availability of career advice from people who had never managed anyone. An MBA gave me something to say when people asked what I was doing. A credential that signals I am still in the game.

But sitting here today, I wonder if that is enough of a reason. If I am just collecting another piece of paper to avoid facing the question of what I actually want from whatever time is left.

Email from a former colleague today. She is now VP at a regional credit union and asked if I would be interested in consulting on a branch optimization project over the summer. The pay is modest but the work is exactly what I know how to do.

I said yes immediately, then spent an hour wondering if I should have negotiated. Old habits. But the truth is I would have done it for free just to be back in a room where people want my opinion, not because of an assignment but because I have answers they need. That probably sounds desperate. Maybe it is.

Nudge: "You said you would have done it for free. What does that tell you about what you're really looking for right now?"

Response: That I need to matter somewhere. The MBA is about credentials, about what looks right on paper. But this — someone calling because they remembered I knew what I was doing — that is about actually being useful. I suppose I have been confusing the two.

End of semester. Submitted my last paper at 11:58 PM. Overall GPA: 3.7. I should feel accomplished. Mostly I feel relieved that I can stop pretending to be something I am not for three months. The consulting project starts Monday.

My sister asked me if I was "doing okay" on the phone last night with that voice she uses when she's actually asking if I'm depressed. I'm not depressed. I made banana bread with Olive yesterday and we painted rocks in the backyard and I reorganized the entire pantry because it was driving me insane. That's not depression, that's Tuesday.

She doesn't get it because she's single and works in marketing and goes to Barcelona on a whim. Good for her. I chose this. I'm not sitting here wishing I had her life. I just wish mine had a few more surprise elements built in. Like a plot twist every now and then. Something I didn't see coming at 6am when I set the cereal bowls out.

Nudge: "What kind of plot twist are you hoping for?"

Response: Honestly? I don't even know. That's the part that scares me. I can't name the thing I want. I just know the absence of it. Like there's a shape missing from my week and I keep filling it with pantry organization and sourdough starters.

Spent three hours at a pumpkin patch watching kids run in circles. I stood there holding juice boxes like a human vending machine. Got home and Kyle asked how my day was and I said "fine" and went to bed at 8:30.

Nudge: "What would've made it better?"

Response: It was fine. Kids had fun. That's the point, right?
Signed up for a ceramics class on Thursday evenings. Kyle said he'd handle bedtime. First class is next week. Already looking forward to getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh.

First ceramics class happened. Small studio off Broadway, eight people, mostly women, a couple retirees, one guy who looked like he was there because of a breakup. The instructor is this woman named Greta who has clay permanently embedded in her fingernails and talks about centering the clay like it's a spiritual practice.

I threw my first cylinder and it collapsed immediately. The second one wobbled but held. Greta said I had "aggressive hands" which I think was a compliment. My nails are ruined and I got clay in my hair and I didn't check my phone once in two hours. Didn't even think about it.

Drove home with the windows down even though it was forty degrees. Played music loud. Walked in the door and the house was quiet, kids asleep, Kyle on the couch watching something about the stock market. He asked if I had fun. I said yes. He said "good" and went back to his show.

I stood in the kitchen eating leftover pasta straight from the container and felt something I haven't felt in a while. Not happiness exactly. More like I'd briefly been someone else. Or maybe someone I used to be.

Nudge: "Someone else, or someone you used to be?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe both. Maybe they're the same thing at this point.

Can't sleep. Kyle is snoring. I'm in the kitchen with the light over the stove and my phone and a glass of wine that I'm pretending is just one glass even though I refilled it.

Looked at flights to Mexico. Not for any reason. Just wanted to see how much it would cost. $340 round trip to Puerto Vallarta. That's less than what I spend on groceries in a week for this family. I could go for a weekend. I won't. But I could.

Scrolled through my old Instagram from 2017, before Olive was born. There's a photo of me at a music festival in Austin with body paint on my arms and a flower crown and I'm laughing so hard my eyes are closed. I don't recognize that person. Or I do, but she feels like a character from a show I watched a long time ago. The plot moved on and she got written out.

The wine is warm now. I should go to bed. Tomorrow is a half day at school for Mason which means I need to figure out lunch and an afternoon activity and probably a trip to Target because he needs new sneakers. Again. Nine-year-old boys destroy shoes like it's a competitive sport.

I just want one day where I don't know what's going to happen next. Where the schedule doesn't exist and I can just follow whatever sounds interesting. Is that so unreasonable?

Nudge: "What would you actually do with that day?"

Response: Drive somewhere I've never been. No map. Stop when something catches my eye. Eat at a restaurant where I can't read the menu. Talk to strangers. Stay out past dark. I've thought about it enough that I have a whole itinerary, which kind of defeats the purpose.

Same night, still up. Kyle got up to use the bathroom and saw me in the kitchen and asked what I was doing. I said "just thinking." He said "about what?" and I said "nothing" and he shrugged and went back to bed. That's the whole conversation. That's what passes for connection at midnight in this house.

I'm not blaming him. He works. He's tired. He handles bedtime when I go to ceramics. He doesn't complain. But he also doesn't ask. Not really. He asks the way you ask a coworker in the elevator. "How was your day?" is not the same as "How are you?" and neither of those is the same as "What do you need?"

Nobody asks me what I need. I'm the person who asks everyone else. What do you want for lunch. Did you brush your teeth. Where's your backpack. Do you need a jacket. I am a logistics coordinator for four other humans and my own needs are somewhere on page twelve of a to-do list that only goes to page three.

I know how this sounds. I know every parent feels this way sometimes. I know I'm not special. I know people have real problems and mine is that I'm bored and restless in a nice house in Littleton, Colorado with a functioning dishwasher and a husband who doesn't yell. I know that. But knowing it doesn't make the feeling go away. It just makes me feel guilty for having it.

Halloween aftermath. Candy wrappers everywhere. Olive had a meltdown over her princess costume ripping. Mason ate too much sugar and threw up. Lucy was fine because Lucy is always fine. I picked glitter out of the carpet for forty minutes.

Second ceramics class. Made a bowl that actually looks like a bowl. Greta said my centering is improving. Hands are sore. Good sore. The kind of tired that means you did something, not the kind that means the day happened to you.

Kyle and I had takeout Thai after the kids went down. He mentioned his office is doing a retreat to Tahoe in January. No partners. I said "sounds fun" and meant it, but also felt something heavy settle in my stomach.

Nudge: "What was the heavy thing?"

Response: Jealousy, I think. Not of Tahoe. Of having something on the calendar that's just for you. That nobody scheduled for you. That you get to anticipate without coordinating childcare first.

Parent-teacher conference for Mason. Teacher said he's doing well in math, needs work on reading comprehension. Brought cupcakes for Lucy's class birthday. Picked up dry cleaning. Groceries. Normal stuff.

Olive got sick, so I missed ceramics. Sat in her room rubbing her back while she slept and scrolled through the class group chat watching everyone post photos of their glazed pieces from last week. Mine is still sitting on the drying shelf. Greta texted me separately and said she'd fire it for me.

I know missing one class isn't a big deal. But it felt like something being taken away. Like the universe reminding me that I don't actually get to have my own thing. Every time I carve out something that's mine, a fever or a school closure or a dentist appointment swallows it back up. I sat there watching Olive breathe and felt both tender and trapped at the same time.

Nudge: "Can you hold both of those at once?"

My college friend Dana visited this weekend. She lives in Portland, runs a food truck, just got back from a month in Vietnam. She showed up with good wine and stories about motorbike rides through rice paddies and a tattoo she got from a guy in Hanoi. The kids loved her because she's loud and funny and let them paint her toenails.

After they went to bed she asked me the question everyone eventually asks: "Do you miss working?" And I gave my usual answer which is that I don't miss the job, I miss the energy. The feeling of making something happen. The chaos of event day when everything could go wrong and you're the one holding it together with duct tape and charm.

She said I should start planning events again, freelance. Small stuff. Birthday parties, local festivals. I said maybe. She said "you always say maybe" and she's right. I do always say maybe. Maybe is how you say no without having to defend it.

But here's what I didn't tell Dana. I don't want to plan other people's good times anymore. I want to have my own. I want to be the person at the festival, not the person who booked the porta-potties. I want to be surprised by something. I want to walk into a room and not know what's about to happen. That's what I miss. Not the logistics. The feeling.

Kyle wouldn't understand that. He'd say I can go to a festival anytime. He'd buy me tickets and think he'd solved the problem. But it's not about the ticket. It's about the fact that somewhere along the way, I became the kind of person who needs permission to enjoy things, and I don't know how to undo that.

Nudge: "Who are you asking permission from?"

Response: Everyone. No one. Myself, probably. I don't know. It's just easier to say maybe and keep making lunches.

Another steering committee meeting where half the room was on their phones. I run a tight agenda and still can't get people to care about infrastructure decisions that will haunt us for years. Nobody wants to own anything hard anymore.

Nudge: "What does it cost you when they check out like that?"

Response: It makes me wonder why I bother holding the line if nobody else will.

Found out today they picked Raj for the VP role. The announcement went out at 2pm in a company-wide email. I heard about it from my skip-level before that, which I guess was supposed to soften the blow. Raj has been here six years. I have been here nineteen.

I am not saying he is unqualified. He ran the platform migration last year and it went well. But I have been running the reliability council since before he joined the company. I built the incident response process from nothing. Three directors told me privately that they assumed the role was mine. So either those conversations meant nothing or something changed that nobody told me about.

Had a one-on-one with my manager afterward. She said the decision came down to "organizational energy" and "forward-looking vision." I asked what that meant concretely and she pivoted to talking about how much they value my technical depth. That phrase again. Technical depth. It is starting to feel like a box they put you in when they do not see you leading.

Nudge: "Those three directors who assumed it was yours -- did any of them advocate for you?"

Response: I actually do not know. I assumed they did but maybe assuming was the whole problem.
Spent most of Saturday reviewing the Q3 reliability targets. Updated my tracking sheet for the team. Chen is slipping again, two missed deadlines this month.

Cannot stop thinking about the VP thing. Told myself I was over it but then I saw Raj's new title in the org chart and my chest got tight. Thirty years and I am still a "senior individual contributor." Hate that I care this much.

Nudge: "You said you hate that you care. What would it look like if you didn't?"

Response: Honestly I have no idea. I do not know who I am if I stop keeping score.

Weird thing happened at the barbecue yesterday. Tom from next door, he retired from Boeing last year, he was talking about building a deck. Not a fancy deck, just a simple one off the back of his kitchen. And he was describing the process of picking the lumber, getting the permits, measuring everything twice, and I realized I was genuinely envious. Not of the deck. Of the way he talked about it. Like it mattered to him for no reason other than he wanted to do it.

I have not built anything with my hands in years. Used to do woodworking in my twenties before the career took off. I still have my dad's old chisels in the garage somewhere, wrapped in an oilcloth. I think about them sometimes.

After the barbecue I went home and sat in the garage for a while. Found the chisels. They were right where I left them, maybe eight years ago. The handles are still good. I did not do anything with them, just held one and felt the weight. Karen came out and asked what I was doing and I said "nothing" which I guess was technically true. But it did not feel like nothing.

Long week. Raj scheduled a "vision alignment" meeting for the whole engineering org and I had to sit through it smiling. He is reorganizing the reliability council under his new structure which means my committee now reports up through him. Three years of building that group and now I am presenting to someone I used to mentor.

Karen noticed I have been staying up late. She asked if I was okay and I said I was fine, just busy. She did not push it. Part of me wanted her to push it.

Nudge: "Why not just tell Karen what's actually going on?"

Spent Sunday morning updating my LinkedIn profile. Added a few bullet points about the incident response framework and the reliability council. Looked at job postings out of curiosity. Principal engineer roles at three companies. Two of them pay more than what I make now.

Did not apply to any of them. Just screenshot the listings and saved them to a folder on my desktop called "Options." Have not decided if that is planning or procrastination.

Nudge: "That folder -- when do you think you will actually open it again?"

Had a skip-level with the CTO today. She asked me to lead a new cross-functional task force on AI readiness. Big scope, high visibility, reports directly to her. On paper it is exactly what I have been wanting. Direct line to the top, chance to shape strategy.

But I keep thinking about why now. Two weeks after passing me over they hand me a consolation project. Raj probably suggested it. I said yes because saying no would look petty. Not sure if I actually want to do it or if I just cannot stand to be seen turning down something important.

Took a half day and went to the hardware store. Bought some walnut stock and a new set of clamps. Nothing serious, just enough to try a small cutting board. Felt strange buying something that has nothing to do with work or credentials or org charts.

Spent the afternoon in the garage planing the wood down. My technique is rusty but the motions came back faster than I expected. I was out there for three hours and did not check my phone once. I cannot remember the last time that happened.

Nudge: "Three hours without your phone. What was going through your head out there?"

Response: Mostly just the grain direction and whether my measurements were right. That was the whole point I think. Just the thing in front of me.

First AI task force meeting was today. I spent all weekend preparing a charter document, twelve pages, frameworks, timelines, resource asks. I presented it and the CTO said "this is thorough" which I used to take as a compliment but now I wonder if it means "too much." She asked if we could "move faster and iterate." That phrase.

Raj sat in on the meeting. He did not say much but he was nodding along like he was evaluating me. Maybe I am reading into it. Probably I am reading into it. But I have been in enough rooms to know when someone is there to observe rather than participate.

Came home and Karen had dinner ready which was nice. She asked about my day and I gave her the short version. She said it sounded like a good opportunity. I agreed. We watched something on TV and I fell asleep on the couch before it ended. She put a blanket on me. I woke up at 2am still on the couch and went upstairs and she was already asleep. These are the kinds of things that keep happening and I am not sure either of us is noticing how much space there is between us now.

Nudge: "That blanket she put on you -- when did you two stop talking about the hard stuff?"

Response: We talk. Just not about the things that would require either of us to change something.

Finished the cutting board. Walnut with maple inlay, nothing fancy but the joints are tight. Rubbed it down with mineral oil and it looks good. Brought it inside and set it on the counter.

Karen picked it up and turned it over a few times. She said "You made this?" and I said yeah. She put it down and said "It's really beautiful, Gerry." Something about the way she said my name. Have not heard her say it like that in a long time.

Second task force meeting went better. I cut the charter down to four pages and proposed a two-week sprint to prototype something instead of planning for another month. The CTO seemed pleased. Raj was not there this time which helped. I could just run the room without wondering if I was being graded.

Chen came to me after the meeting and said he wants to transfer onto the task force. I was surprised. He has been underperforming on my reliability team but maybe that is because he does not care about reliability work. Maybe I have been measuring him against the wrong thing. I told him I would think about it but I think I am going to say yes.

Ordered more walnut and some cherry this time. Thinking about making a small side table next. Karen asked if she could watch me work in the garage sometime and I said sure. We are going to Portland together next weekend. Her idea but I said yes without hesitating which is new. The Options folder is still on my desktop. I have not opened it in two weeks. Not sure what that means yet but it does not bother me the way it probably should.

New Year's Eve and I'm sitting at Erik's kitchen table while everyone else is in the living room watching some countdown special. His uncle was here earlier, going off about how the city council's new housing plan for asylum seekers is going to "ruin the neighbourhood." Nobody said anything. I didn't say anything. Erik kind of half-laughed and changed the subject to football.

I've met some of the families that housing plan is for. I know their names. I know Fatima's daughter just started school and is terrified of the cafeteria. And I just sat there holding my drink and let this man talk about them like they were a problem to be managed. I kept telling myself it wasn't the right moment, it's a party, don't make it weird. But that's just an excuse.

Nudge: "What stopped you more - the setting or something else?"

Response: Scared I'd lose my temper and they'd just dismiss me.

Spent the morning at Akademibokhandeln buying a new notebook for the semester and ended up at the Espresso House on Svartbäcksgatan after. I ordered a coffee and one of those wrapped sandwiches and then just stared at the packaging. Plastic wrap, plastic tray, a paper sleeve that probably isn't even recyclable. I've been reading about single-use packaging policy for my thesis for months. I know the numbers. I know what this stuff does. And I bought it anyway because I was hungry and didn't feel like walking home first.

It sounds ridiculous to feel bad about a sandwich wrapper. People would laugh. But it's the pattern that bugs me. I write about systemic failures in environmental policy and then I make the lazy choice when it's my own lunch. The gap between what I study and how I actually live keeps showing up in these small ways. I don't buy fast fashion anymore, I cycle, I eat less meat than I used to. But then there's this sandwich in a plastic coffin and I think, what's the point of any of it if I can't even get the small stuff right.

My housemate Nils would say I'm overthinking it. He'd say one sandwich wrapper doesn't matter. And he'd be technically correct. But I think the willingness to say "it doesn't matter" about any single thing is exactly how you end up not caring about anything.

Nudge: "Do you actually believe one sandwich wrapper matters, or is it about something bigger?"

Response: It's about whether I'm becoming someone who just talks.
Overheard Markus in the department kitchen making a joke about a Somali student's accent during a presentation. A couple of people laughed. I pretended I was busy with the coffee machine.

I'm so tired. Thesis crunch plus volunteering Saturdays plus a seminar on EU regulatory frameworks that runs three hours every Tuesday evening. I barely have energy to cook. Today I ordered Foodora twice. Both times it was from chains I know treat their delivery riders badly. I've seen the articles. I've shared the articles. And then I opened the app and ordered anyway because I couldn't face making pasta.

It's not that I forgot. I just didn't care enough in the moment. Or I cared but my tiredness won. I don't know which is worse. The riders are out there in January cold on bikes so I don't have to walk to my own kitchen. I can dress it up however I want but that's what happened.

Had a supervision meeting with Professor Lindqvist today. She liked my chapter draft on enforcement gaps but said I need to address the "political feasibility" section more carefully. Fair enough. But then she casually mentioned that the department is being courted by Vattenfall for a research partnership and asked if I'd be interested in being a student liaison. Good for my CV, she said. Good connections.

Vattenfall is state-owned and technically pivoting toward renewables, but they also ran lignite mines in Germany for decades and their track record on indigenous Sámi land rights in the north is awful. I know this because I wrote a case study on it last year. Lindqvist knows I wrote it. She was my supervisor for that paper.

I should have said no on the spot. Instead I said I'd think about it. Because part of me is thinking about what it would look like on a job application. Part of me is doing the math on whether working with them from the inside might actually lead to something useful. And I hate that I'm even calculating like this, because I know that's how everyone justifies it. "Change from within." It's the oldest excuse in the book and I've heard it a hundred times from people I don't respect. But here I am, considering it for myself, because Professor Lindqvist smiled when she said my name and I liked being chosen.

Nudge: "What bothers you more - the partnership or that you liked being asked?"

Response: Both. But mostly the second one.

Still thinking about the Vattenfall thing from this morning. Went for a walk along the river after dinner to try to sort it out and I'm not sure I managed. The thing is, I talk a lot about how corporations co-opt well-meaning people. I literally have a slide in my thesis presentation about it. "Institutional capture of reform narratives." That's my phrase. And now I'm being offered a small taste of exactly that.

Convenience is the word I keep coming back to. It would be convenient for my career. It would be convenient to tell myself I'm doing it for the right reasons. It would be convenient to let Professor Lindqvist handle the ethical questions while I just show up and take notes and put Vattenfall on my LinkedIn. That's what most people would do. That's what a practical person would do.

But I keep thinking about Fatima and her daughter from the refugee centre, and the Sámi reindeer herders whose grazing land got destroyed for a mine that Vattenfall then sold off when it became unprofitable. These are not abstract policy questions to me. Or at least they shouldn't be. If I take this liaison position, I become someone who files those things under "complicated" and moves on. And I've always told myself I wouldn't be that person. I just didn't expect the test to come from my own supervisor.

Nudge: "Have you decided, or are you still hoping you won't have to?"

I told Lindqvist I'd pass on the Vattenfall liaison position. She took it well enough, said she understood, moved on to thesis feedback. I don't think she was surprised.

At the volunteer session on Saturday, one of the newer volunteers, a guy named Anders, made a comment about how some of the refugee families "don't even try to learn Swedish." He said it lightly, like it was just an observation. Two other volunteers nodded. I know for a fact that three of the families he's talking about attend SFI classes four mornings a week. One of the mothers, Amira, practices Swedish vocabulary with her kids at the kitchen table every evening. I've seen her notebook.

I didn't correct him. I thought about it. I opened my mouth and then just said something about how the language courses have long waiting lists, which is true but wasn't really the point. The point was that he was wrong and I sidestepped it instead of saying so directly. I keep doing this thing where I provide context instead of actually challenging what someone says. It feels diplomatic in the moment but afterward it just feels like cowardice dressed up as nuance.

Quiet week. Spent most of it in the library working through policy documents for chapter four. The snow has been heavy and cycling has been miserable but the bus is worse, so I keep at it. Had fika with Nils and our other housemate Sara on Wednesday. We talked about summer plans. Nils wants to do a hiking trip in Norway. Sara is going home to Malmö.

Cooked a big pot of lentil soup on Sunday that I've been eating all week. My mum called to check in, asked about the thesis timeline. I told her it's on track for June submission. She sounded relieved. Nothing eventful really. Just the steady rhythm of getting through January.

Thesis stress is properly hitting now. I was up until two in the morning reading IPCC reports and cross-referencing enforcement data and at some point I just stopped and looked at what I was doing. I'm writing about policy mechanisms that might reduce emissions by some fraction of a percentage point over a decade. And meanwhile the numbers keep getting worse. Every single year. I read a paper last week that said we've already locked in enough warming to make certain Pacific islands uninhabitable. Already locked in. Past tense.

So what am I actually doing. I'm sitting in a well-heated Swedish apartment writing about governance frameworks while people are losing their homes to rising seas right now. My thesis will get graded, filed, maybe cited in another thesis. The policy recommendations I'm writing will be read by my supervisor and two examiners and then forgotten. That's the honest truth of it.

I could have gone into something more direct. Field work. Activism. Legal advocacy. Instead I chose the comfortable academic route where I get to feel smart about the problem without ever getting my hands dirty. My friend Elsa works for an NGO in Bangladesh building flood shelters. She makes a third of what I'll make when I graduate. She's also actually doing something. I chose to write about the problem from a distance because it's safer and it counts toward a degree. That's the choice I made today and every day I sit in that library.

Nudge: "Is the thesis actually the problem, or is it that you're not sure it's enough?"

Response: I don't know. I'm too tired to answer that honestly right now.

Came back to write more after lying in bed for an hour not sleeping. There was a moment at the volunteer centre last week where someone asked me to help draft a complaint letter about housing conditions and I said I was too busy with my thesis. I wasn't too busy. I just didn't want to take it on.

Nudge: "What made you not want to take it on?"

Response: Honestly? I just didn't want another thing to carry.

I've been thinking about something all week. On Tuesday I went to the supermarket and I was standing in front of the coffee shelf trying to decide between the Fairtrade one that costs almost double and the store brand. I always buy the Fairtrade one. It's one of those small choices I make that lets me tell myself I'm someone who pays attention. But this time I picked up the store brand and put it in my basket. I was over budget for the month and I just thought, I can't afford to care about this right now. And then I stood there for a solid minute feeling sick about a bag of coffee.

It's the same pattern from the sandwich wrapper in January. These tiny consumer decisions that I load with all this meaning. But maybe the meaning is real. If I stop caring about the coffee, do I stop caring about the next thing, and the thing after that. At what point does "I can't afford it right now" become a permanent excuse. I watched my dad do this over years. He used to be involved with Greenpeace, went to protests, the whole thing. Now he drives an SUV and says the protests don't accomplish anything anymore. He didn't change overnight. It was a thousand small surrenders.

I don't want that to be me. But I also bought the cheap coffee. And I drank it this morning and it tasted fine and I didn't think about it until now. That's the part that scares me. How quickly the guilt fades. How easily convenience becomes the default. I keep writing in this thing like that's going to help, but I'm not sure documenting my failures counts as doing something about them.

Today was the monthly temple committee meeting and I just cannot shake this strange feeling. Meena aunty said something about how "some people like to take charge of everything" and I know she was talking about me. Everyone was looking at their phones or the floor. I have been running this committee for six years and we have never had a failed event, never run out of food, never had volunteers not show up. But suddenly I am the problem?

I stayed quiet. What else could I do? If I defended myself it would look exactly like what she was accusing me of. But sitting there pretending I did not hear her felt like swallowing glass. My husband said I should just ignore it, that Meena aunty is just jealous because her daughter-in-law moved her out of the main house. Maybe. But that does not explain why no one else spoke up for me.

The thing is, I take charge because if I do not, things fall apart. Remember the Holi celebration last year when I was at Ravi's parents' house? Complete disaster. Cold food, wrong decorations, crying children. But sure, I am the one who is too controlling.

Nudge: "What held you back from responding?"

Dropped off three containers of biryani to the Sharma family down the street. Their son just had surgery and the mother looked like she had not slept in days. She cried when she saw me at the door. This is what it is all about, really. Showing up when people need you.

Nudge: "How did it feel when she cried?"

Response: Honestly, it reminded me of when Amma had her first stroke and neighbors brought us food for weeks. You never forget who showed up. I want to be that person for others. Sometimes I think this is the only thing I am truly good at.

Priya came home crying from school because her friend group excluded her from a birthday party. I spent an hour consoling her but the whole time I kept thinking about that committee meeting. Is this what it feels like? To be pushed out? Too tired to think about this more.

Nudge: "Are you thinking about yourself or Priya?"

Response: Priya, obviously. She is thirteen and everything feels like the end of the world at that age. I am just tired.

Ravi's sister called asking if we can host his parents for six weeks instead of two this summer. She has a work trip to Singapore that "cannot be rescheduled." I said yes before even thinking about it. What else could I say?

Now I am mentally reorganizing everything. Priya's room will have to be cleared for them. I will need to cook separate low-sodium meals for Appa. The bathroom schedule becomes a nightmare with seven people. But Lakshmi made it sound like I was the only solution, and I suppose I am.

Nudge: "Did you want to say something else?"

Response: Wanting and doing are different things. Lakshmi works, I do not. She has meetings in Singapore, I have school drop-offs. The math is simple. But yes, fine, I wanted to say "let me check with Ravi first" like a normal person. Instead I just absorbed it like always.

Nothing happened today, just the usual. But I have been thinking about something my cousin Anita said when she visited last month. She asked why I "gave up" my career to stay home, as if I am sitting here watching television all day. I manage a household, a family, a community. I coordinate schedules for five people, handle three different school systems' requirements, manage all the finances, plan every festival, and support whoever needs me.

Ravi could not find his passport last week. Who found it in twenty minutes? Me. Priya needed a costume for her Bharatanatyam recital. Who stayed up sewing until midnight? Me. Vikram's college applications needed someone to proofread seven essays. Me.

But because there is no salary, it is not real work. Because I do not have a LinkedIn profile anymore, I have "given up." Anita has a corner office and takes two vacations a year. Good for her. She also calls her mother once a month and sends birthday cards late. I am not saying one is better. I am just saying they are different, and I am tired of having to defend my choice.

Committee met again. I volunteered to step back from leading the Navratri planning. Let someone else handle the vendor contracts and volunteer schedules. Meena aunty looked surprised. Everyone did, actually.

Nudge: "How did it feel to step back?"

Made chai for myself this afternoon and sat on the balcony for fifteen minutes doing nothing. The house was quiet. Just me and the crows arguing in the mango tree. Cannot remember the last time I did this.

Nudge: "What made today different?"

Vikram called from university just to talk. Not because he needed money or help with something. Just to tell me about his professor who reminds him of his grandfather, and about the dosa place he found near campus. We talked for forty-five minutes.

I think I did something right with him. He is kind. He notices people. Last month he organized a food drive at his dorm because he saw international students struggling during spring break when the dining hall closed. He did not tell me about it - his roommate's mother mentioned it. That is the part that makes me proudest. He does not do it for recognition.

Nudge: "What do you think shaped him that way?"

Response: I wish I could take credit but honestly, I think he just watched. Watched me drop off food to the Sharmas. Watched his grandmother live with us for three years. Watched Ravi stay late at office but still make every recital and cricket match. Children learn what they see, not what you tell them.

Second entry today. In-laws arrived this evening, four days early. Lakshmi's flight got moved up. I had just finished cleaning but the guest room mattress has not been aired. Amma looked at the sheets and I know she noticed. Too tired to care right now.

Amma has been here four days and I am already losing my mind. She reorganized my spice cabinet. MY spice cabinet that I have arranged the same way for fifteen years. She said she was "helping" because it was "inefficient." I have managed three children, a husband, a house, and an entire temple committee with that "inefficient" cabinet.

I did not say anything. What would be the point? She is seventy-two and she is a guest. But Ravi saw my face when I opened the cabinet door and he just shrugged. Shrugged! As if his mother rearranging my kitchen is completely normal and I should just accept it.

The worst part is that she is not wrong. Her arrangement probably is more efficient. But that is not the point. The point is that this is my home, my space, the one domain where I should have some say. But apparently even here I am meant to just smile and adjust. I made everyone's favorite dinner anyway because what else am I going to do, let everyone starve over cumin placement?

Nudge: "What would having a say look like?"

Response: It does not matter. Four more weeks and then things go back to normal. I just need to survive until then.

Priya asked me today why I always have to be "in charge of everything." She was annoyed because I reminded her three times about her homework. Three times! Because she was on her phone and the deadline was in two hours. But apparently I am controlling.

I told her that someone has to keep track of things. She rolled her eyes and said "you could just let me fail once." What kind of mother lets her child fail on purpose? I did not hover over Vikram and look how he turned out - responsible, organized, successful. But Priya is different. She needs more reminders. If I do not push her, she will coast.

Ravi thinks I should "give her space." Easy for him to say. He is at office twelve hours a day. He does not see the daily negotiation of getting this child to do anything. When she fails a test, no one will ask where her father was. They will ask what I was doing.

But maybe everyone is right and I am the problem. Maybe I should just stop caring. Let the laundry pile up, let the homework go undone, let the spice cabinet stay however Amma wants it. Then we will see how things run.

Nudge: "What would happen if you did stop?"

Response: Chaos. Or maybe nothing. Maybe everything would be fine and I would realize I have been making myself essential when I am actually just in the way. That thought is worse than the chaos, honestly.

Amma and I had an argument today. Or not an argument exactly - she does not argue. She just makes observations. "In my house we always did it this way." "You work so hard, beta, but maybe if you organized differently..." "Priya seems stressed, have you thought about letting her breathe?"

I finally said something. I told her that I appreciate her concern but this is my house and my family and I have been managing fine for twenty years. She got very quiet and went to her room. Ravi says I was too harsh. Maybe. But she has been here two weeks and every day there is a new suggestion, a new correction, a new way I am falling short.

The thing is, she ran her own household for forty years. I understand the need to feel useful, to have a role. When she moved in with Lakshmi she lost all of that. So she comes here and tries to find her place by improving mine. I see the pattern. I am not blind to it.

But seeing the pattern does not make it easier to live with. And I keep wondering - is this my future? Will I be the mother-in-law who cannot stop fixing things because fixing things is all I know how to do? Will Priya write journal entries about me reorganizing her kitchen?

Nudge: "What would you want to be remembered for?"

Response: Not for being right. Not for having the most organized spice cabinet or the best-run committee. I want Vikram and Priya and even little Arun to remember that I showed up. That when things fell apart, I was there. That I fed people not because I needed to control the menu but because I believe you cannot love someone properly if you let them go hungry. That is why I do all of this. I think I forgot that somewhere along the way.

My mother-in-law rearranged Sami's entire nap schedule without asking me. She told Tariq it was better for the baby to sleep earlier so she could take him to the park in the afternoon. Nobody asked what I thought. I had finally gotten him into a rhythm that gave me two hours in the evening to work on a logo project. That's gone now.

I stood there in the kitchen holding a cup of tea and I just didn't say anything. Tariq looked at me like he expected me to agree. His mother was already moving the crib blanket to the other room. I could feel my jaw clenching but I smiled. I hate that I smiled. Something about the way everyone just decided felt like they forgot I'm his mother, not a guest in this arrangement.

Nudge: "You smiled but didn't want to. What stopped you?"

Sami had a fever all night. I was up every two hours. Tariq slept through it. His mother came in at 5am and took over, told me to rest. I should be grateful but it felt like being dismissed. I'm the one who knows what dose of medicine to give, what temperature means we go to the clinic. I didn't rest.

Nudge: "What would the right kind of help look like?"

Response: If she had asked. Just asked. "Can I sit with him so you can sleep?" instead of walking in and lifting him out of my arms. It's not the help. It's that nobody checks if I want it first.

Sami's fever broke. Normal day otherwise. Did laundry, made lentil soup, swept the hallway because nobody else notices the dust. Tariq came home and asked what's for dinner. I said soup. He said his mother already made something. So the soup is in the fridge now, I guess.

Finished a draft of the logo I've been working on. The client wants revisions but at least the base concept is solid. I had to work on it in the bathroom because Sami was asleep in the bedroom and the living room was occupied. Sat on the edge of the bathtub with my laptop balanced on the hamper. It's ridiculous but it's the only place I can close a door without anyone asking what I'm doing.

Can barely keep my eyes open. Sami is teething and it's been three nights of broken sleep. I had to email the client and ask for an extension on the logo revisions. Felt pathetic typing that out. My mother called to say my cousin Rania is getting engaged and when am I bringing Sami to visit. Everything requires something from me and I have nothing left to give right now.

Eid preparations are consuming the entire house. Tariq's mother has been cooking since dawn. She made ma'moul and knafeh and keeps sending me to the store for things she forgot. I don't mind the errands but she keeps correcting how I arrange the pastries on the tray. Told me that's not how they do it in their family. It's been two years, I still arrange things wrong apparently.

Tariq gave me money to buy a new outfit for Eid. I picked something I liked -- a dark green dress with embroidery on the sleeves. When I showed it to his mother she said it was pretty but maybe something more "appropriate for a young mother" would be better. I don't even know what that means. I put the dress in my closet anyway. I'll wear it.

My own mother sent a voice note about how she wishes I could spend Eid at their house this year. First Eid is always with the husband's family, she knows that, but she said it anyway. So now I feel guilty about being here and guilty about not being there. Sami won't remember any of this. I'm doing all of it for people who have opinions about trays and dresses.

Nudge: "You said you'll wear the dress anyway. What's riding on that?"

Response: It's just a dress. I liked it, I bought it, I'll wear it. Not everything has to be a statement.

Later. Wore the dress. Tariq said I looked nice. His mother didn't comment. My sister-in-law Huda actually asked where I got it. Sami grabbed the embroidery and pulled a thread loose, which honestly made me laugh. Small thing but I felt like myself for the first time in weeks. I held Sami on my hip and I felt steady.

Got the payment for the logo project. Not a lot -- 45 JD -- but I earned it. Put it in the envelope I keep in my underwear drawer. I have about 200 JD saved now. I don't know what I'm saving for exactly. Maybe a better laptop. Maybe just proof that I can do something that isn't cooking and wiping noses.

Tariq doesn't know about the envelope. I'm not hiding it because I think he'd be angry, more because he'd want to "help manage it" and then it wouldn't feel like mine anymore. My father always handled my mother's money. She'd get her teacher's salary and hand it over and he'd decide what the household needed. She never complained about it. I think about that a lot. I don't want to not complain about it. I want a reason not to need to.

Nudge: "What changes if Tariq finds that envelope?"

Response: He'd probably be fine with it. Supportive even. But then it becomes a shared thing, a family decision. Right now it's the only thing in this house that belongs to just me. I'm not ready to give that up. Maybe that's selfish. I don't care.

Took Sami to the park near the mosque this morning. He's getting better at walking on his own, barely needs my hand now. He kept crouching down to look at ants. We stayed for almost two hours. I didn't bring my phone on purpose.

Ran into Um Fadi from the building. She asked if I was working these days, meaning a real job. I said no, just home with Sami. She nodded the way people nod when they think they're being polite but their face says everything. I used to let that bother me. Today I just picked Sami up and pointed at a bird and we moved on.

Tariq's mother made mansaf for dinner and I helped her roll the jameed. She showed me her mother's technique with the wooden spoon and for once it wasn't corrective, it was like she was sharing something. I actually paid attention. Her hands move fast for someone her age. The rice came out perfectly and she said "you're learning" and I think she meant it warmly. These moments confuse me because they make me forget why I'm frustrated. Or maybe they're exactly why I stay patient through the rest of it.

Friday. Tariq took the day off and we went to his parents' village with Sami to visit his grandfather. The old house has a courtyard with a lemon tree that Tariq's grandmother planted. His grandfather told Sami the same story he tells everyone about building the house stone by stone. Sami just chewed on a lemon leaf.

I sat with the women while the men did their thing. Tariq's aunt made sage tea the old way, on a brass tray over coals. It was good. Quiet. I sketched some patterns on a napkin -- the tile work in the courtyard is beautiful, these geometric interlocking shapes I've been wanting to use in a design project. Nobody noticed me drawing, which was the whole point.

New client reached out through Instagram. Wants a logo for a women's fitness studio in West Amman. Paying 80 JD. I said yes before thinking about when I'd actually do the work. Sami's not sleeping well again and the house is never empty. But 80 JD is 80 JD. I'll figure out the hours somehow. I always do.

Nudge: "You keep saying you'll figure it out. Figure out what exactly?"

Response: How to be two people at once. The one who takes care of Sami and the house and keeps everyone comfortable. And the one who wants to make things and get paid for it and have something that's mine. There's no schedule that fits both. I just keep squeezing one into the gaps of the other.

Tariq and I had an actual conversation last night. Not a fight. He asked why I've been staying up so late and I almost lied but I was too tired to come up with something so I told him about the freelance work. All of it. The clients, the envelope, the bathroom laptop sessions.

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said he wished I'd told him sooner. Not because he wanted to control it but because he felt stupid not knowing. That stung. I told him it wasn't about trust, it was about needing something that was just mine. He said he understood that but it still felt like I didn't trust him. I don't know if he's wrong.

We didn't resolve anything exactly. He said he'd talk to his mother about giving me more space during the day so I don't have to hide. I told him I don't want him to fight that battle for me. He looked confused. I think he genuinely doesn't understand why I'd rather struggle alone than have him fix it. Honestly, I'm not totally sure I understand it either. But the thought of his mother knowing about the work and having opinions about it makes my chest tight. Some things I need to handle my own way, even if my way is harder.

Nudge: "He offered help and you said no. What are you protecting?"

Response: Can we not do this right now. I already spent all night turning this over in my head. I know it doesn't make total sense. I just need to sit with it for a bit.

Same day, later. Tariq's mother asked me at dinner if everything was okay between us. She must have heard us talking last night. I said everything was fine. Tariq backed me up. She didn't push it. But the way she looked at me -- not unkind, just knowing -- made me feel exposed. I keep building walls in a house made of thin ones.

Board meeting this morning. Van der Meer had the nerve to suggest we bring in an outside consultant for the digital transformation project. In front of everyone. I built this company when he was still in university, and now he thinks we need some McKinsey type to tell us how to run our own systems.

I shut it down, maybe too hard. Maarten pulled me aside after and said I was "being territorial." Territorial. As if protecting what you've built for thirty years is some kind of character flaw. The company nearly got swallowed six months ago because I trusted people who turned out to be positioning themselves for the takeover. So yes, I'm careful about who gets their hands on strategy now.

Nudge: "What did it feel like when Maarten used that word?"

Response: Maarten's been with me eighteen years. He earned the right to say what he thinks. That's not the issue. The issue is that he might be right about how it looked in the room. I don't need to look reasonable to people who haven't earned a seat at that table, but Maarten — he's seen me navigate worse than this. If he's worried, maybe I'm letting the takeover mess cloud my judgment. Maybe. I still think van der Meer was out of line.

Can't sleep. Keep thinking about Pieter and Thomas leaving. We worked together for fifteen years. Fifteen years, and they walked into the enemy camp without so much as a conversation first. What does that say about what I built? What does that say about loyalty? Or was it all just leverage, waiting for a better offer?

Had dinner with my daughter Annelies tonight. She asked when I'm going to retire. I told her the same thing I always tell her — when I'm ready. She laughed and said I've been saying that for ten years. She's not wrong.

The truth is, I don't know what I would do. The company isn't just a business, it's the structure of my days. It's where I matter. When I walk into that building, people listen. They need my decisions. At home, Marijke has her garden club and her grandchildren schedule and I'm just another person underfoot. She loves me, I know that, but she doesn't need me the way the company does.

Annelies suggested I mentor young entrepreneurs. The idea of sitting in coffee shops giving advice to twenty-five-year-olds who think they invented disruption makes me want to throw something. I don't want to be an elder statesman. I want to be in the fight.

Nudge: "You said the company is where you matter. What happens if that changes?"

Response: Then I become irrelevant. I've seen it happen to others — men who built empires and then got shuffled off to advisory roles where no one returns their calls. They sit on nonprofit boards and pretend it's the same. It's not the same. The respect you get when you can make things happen is different from the respect you get for what you did thirty years ago. I'm not ready to become a memory while I'm still alive.

Conference in Rotterdam. Ran into Pieter at the networking reception. He had the audacity to come over and shake my hand like nothing happened. "No hard feelings, Henrik. It was just business." Just business. I poured years into developing him. Gave him his first management role when everyone said he was too young. And he used my playbook to compete against me.

I kept my composure. Smiled. Said something about wishing him well. But I was calculating the whole time — who in that room saw us talking, what it looked like, whether he gained anything from being seen with me. This is what it's come to. I can't even have a genuine reaction anymore because every interaction is a positioning exercise.

Back in the hotel room. Skipped the dinner. Couldn't stomach another three hours of strategic smiling. Ordered room service and watched the ceiling. Thirty years and I still have to play these games. When does it end? When do I get to just be somewhere without worrying about who's watching?

Nudge: "When was the last time you weren't performing?"

Quarterly review. Numbers are solid — 8% growth despite the disruption. Told the team they exceeded expectations. Van der Meer apologized for the consultant suggestion. I accepted graciously. Maarten gave me a nod from across the room. Small victories. The company is steady. That's what matters.

Strange moment today. Young account manager named Sophie presented a new client pitch to me. She was nervous — I could see her hands trembling on her laptop. I remembered being that nervous once, thirty-five years ago, presenting to the board of a shipping company we desperately needed as a client.

I let her finish without interrupting, which is not my usual approach. When she was done I told her the pitch was solid but she needed to slow down and own the silences. She looked at me like I'd handed her a map. That felt good. Different from closing a deal, but good.

Maybe Annelies isn't entirely wrong about the mentoring thing. Just not with strangers in coffee shops.

Nudge: "This sounds different from the coffee shop comment. What changed?"

Response: Sophie works for me. She's inside the system I built. If she gets better, the company gets better. That's not the same as dispensing wisdom to random founders who will forget my name in a week. With Sophie, I have context. I know what she's walking into. And honestly, I have stake in her success — if she lands this client, it's a win for us. So maybe mentoring only works when I still have skin in the game. Pure generosity has never been my strength.

Family dinner at Annelies's house. My grandson Lucas asked me what I do all day. I started explaining logistics operations and he interrupted to say his friend's grandfather is retired and takes him fishing every Saturday. Annelies jumped in before I could respond, changed the subject to dessert.

Later Marijke told me I looked hurt. I wasn't hurt. I was annoyed. The comparison is absurd — some retired accountant who goes fishing isn't building something. He's killing time. But I kept thinking about it on the drive home. Lucas is eight. He doesn't know the difference between building and coasting. All he knows is his friend's grandfather has time for him and his doesn't.

The thing is, I could make time. I choose not to because Saturday mornings are when I review the weekly reports uninterrupted. But when I try to explain that maintaining what I've built requires constant attention, even to myself, it starts to sound like an excuse. I'm not sure when protecting the company became a reason to miss everything else.

Nudge: "What would happen if you missed one Saturday of reports?"

Response: Nothing would collapse. I know that. The company survived two weeks when I had my knee surgery. But that's not the point — the point is that I need to know everything. If I don't read those reports, I'm making decisions blind. Someone could be slipping, a client could be unhappy, and I wouldn't catch it until it became a real problem. That's how you lose control.

But Marijke asked me once what I was controlling, exactly. Eighty employees who know their jobs. Systems I set up years ago. Maybe I'm not controlling anything. Maybe I'm just afraid of what happens when I let go.

Woke up at 4 AM thinking about succession. If I died tomorrow, who runs this? Maarten could handle operations but he doesn't have the network. Van der Meer has ambition but I don't trust his judgment yet. Sophie is twenty years from being ready. The honest answer is there's no one, and that's my fault because I never let anyone get close enough to the core.

I tell myself I'm protecting the company from incompetence but maybe I'm protecting myself from being replaced. If someone else can do what I do, then what am I? Just the guy whose name is on the building. I've spent thirty years making sure I was irreplaceable and now that's the problem.

Called Maarten this afternoon. Asked him if he'd be interested in taking on more strategic responsibility. Not as my replacement — as my partner in running the place. He was quiet for a long time. Then he said, "I've been waiting ten years for you to ask." That stung. I thought I was being careful. Turns out I was being a bottleneck.

Nudge: "What made today different?"

Response: The 4 AM panic. And honestly, Lucas asking about fishing. Eight years old and he's already writing me off as the grandfather who's always working. I don't want to be that. I also don't want to hand over everything and become invisible. Sharing with Maarten feels like a middle path — I stay involved but I don't have to carry it alone. Whether I can actually let him carry it without micromanaging, that's another question. But I made the call. That's something.

First day of Maarten handling the morning operations meeting without me. Sat in my office watching the clock. Didn't intervene. Reports came in normal. Nothing fell apart. Went home at 5 PM for the first time in months. Marijke asked if I was feeling alright. I told her I was trying something new.

Took Lucas fishing this morning. Marijke nearly fell over when I told her. I had no idea what I was doing — had to watch a YouTube video on how to bait a hook. Lucas thought that was hilarious. "Opa doesn't know everything!" He's right. I don't.

We sat on the dock for three hours and caught nothing. He told me about his school, his best friend who moved away, the video game he's trying to beat. I listened without trying to teach him anything or steer the conversation. Just listened. At one point he leaned against me and said this was fun. That was worth more than any contract I've ever closed.

On the drive home I thought about what I've been protecting all these years. The company, yes. But also this version of myself that only exists in boardrooms — the one who knows all the answers, who never looks foolish, who everyone defers to. That version doesn't know how to fish. Doesn't have time to learn. Doesn't get to sit with his grandson and be surprised by what a eight-year-old thinks about the world.

I'm not retiring. I'm not ready. But I'm starting to see that there might be more than one way to matter.

Had the tech lead conversation with Martijn today. He laid out what the role would look like — running standups, making architecture calls, representing the team in cross-department meetings. I said I was interested, which is true, but the whole time I was thinking about how I'd actually handle it. I build things well when people leave me alone to think. That's not what this role is.

But I also know I did the right thing saying yes. Everyone on the team expects it, Noor would think I was insane to turn it down, and frankly it would look odd to pass on a promotion you're being handed. I can figure out the rest as I go. Probably.

Nudge: "What part of it actually interests you?"

Long week. Spent most of it pairing with the new hire, walking her through the codebase and our deployment pipeline. Didn't get to touch my own backlog at all but she's ramping up faster than the last person we onboarded so I'll take it.

Nudge: "Was it worth not having your own time?"

Sprint retro got a bit heated. Devika suggested we rethink the approval flow for deployments because she thinks it slows us down. She's right, technically — it does add a half-day lag. I had a whole alternative sketched in my head while she was talking, a lighter-weight check that would still catch the critical stuff.

Didn't say it though. The senior architects set up the current flow and they were in the call. Felt like bringing up a redesign right there would come across as me trying to show off, especially with the tech lead thing floating around. So I just nodded and said I could look into Devika's point offline. She seemed a bit deflated that nobody backed her up more directly. I get it.

Weird day. Started normal — coffee, standup, tickets. Then around lunch Pieter from platform team pinged me about a side project he's been hacking on, this internal tool for tracking deployment health across services. He wanted to know if I'd help build the backend. Not sanctioned work, just something he thinks would be useful and wants to pitch to leadership once it's built.

First thought was yes obviously, this is the kind of thing I actually want to spend time on, building something new without twelve layers of sign-off. Second thought was that Martijn would not love me spending cycles on an unsanctioned project right when he's evaluating me for lead. Third thought was that Pieter didn't ask anyone else, he asked me, and that felt good in a way I don't fully understand. Like someone saw the part of me that wants to just build.

I told Pieter I'd think about it over the weekend. Which is code for "I want to do it but I need to figure out if I'm allowed to want to do it." That sounds ridiculous written down. I'm a grown man deciding whether to write some code on the side. But it feels more loaded than that right now.

Told Pieter I'd help with the tool. We set up a repo and I spent an hour last night after Noor went to sleep scaffolding the API layer. Felt more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint on the actual work.

Today was back to normal though — reviewing PRs, answering Slack threads, sitting through a planning session where half the decisions had already been made before we walked in. The usual. I'm not complaining, it's fine. I just noticed the contrast, that's all.

Nudge: "You said it's fine twice now. Is it?"

Response: Yeah. It's work, it doesn't all have to be exciting.

Noor and I went to dinner with her parents in Rotterdam on Saturday. Her dad asked about the promotion and I gave the standard answer — it's in progress, I'm grateful for the opportunity, etc. He seemed pleased. On the train back Noor said she was proud of me and I felt genuinely good about it. Like I'm on a track that makes sense to the people I care about.

Then on Sunday I stayed up way too late working on Pieter's tool and got completely absorbed refactoring the data ingestion layer. Noor asked what I was doing and I said "just work stuff" which wasn't really a lie but wasn't the whole truth either. I don't know why I didn't just explain it. Maybe because explaining it would mean admitting how much more alive that project makes me feel, and then someone might ask what that says about everything else.

Nudge: "What would it mean if you did explain it to her?"

Response: Probably nothing bad. She'd understand. I think the issue is more that saying it out loud makes it real — that the path everyone's excited about for me isn't the one that lights me up. And then I'd have to figure out what to do with that.

Martijn officially put me on the lead shortlist. Two candidates, me and someone from the other squad. I thanked him and said I'd put my best foot forward. Which I will. Whatever doubts I have aren't anyone else's problem to carry.

Team drinks after work. Everyone was congratulating me about the shortlist like it's already decided. I kept saying it wasn't confirmed yet but nobody listens. Devika made a joke about me being her future boss and I laughed but it sat wrong with me for the rest of the evening.

I don't think I'm being fake about it. I genuinely want to do well, and the role comes with better pay, more visibility, things that matter. But there's this gap between how I talk about it to other people and what actually runs through my head at 11pm when I'm staring at Pieter's repo. Not sure what to do with that. For now I'm doing both, which is sustainable enough.

Presented the deployment health tool to Pieter's skip-level manager today. She liked it. Asked if we'd want to bring it under the platform roadmap officially. Said yes without hesitating.

Nudge: "That was quick for someone who usually thinks things over."

Response: Some things don't need overthinking.

Martijn pulled me aside after standup. Apparently someone mentioned to him that I've been spending time on Pieter's tool and he wanted to know if it was affecting my sprint commitments. It isn't — I've been keeping my velocity up — but the way he framed it made it sound like a warning. "Just want to make sure your priorities are clear," he said.

I told him it wouldn't be an issue and that the tool is now being folded into the platform roadmap anyway, so it's legitimate work. He nodded and dropped it. But I've been replaying the conversation all afternoon. The rational part of me knows he was just doing his job. The other part feels like I got caught doing something I wasn't supposed to enjoy. I hate that I felt the need to justify it. I hate more that I did justify it, immediately, without pushing back at all.

Nudge: "What would pushing back have looked like?"

Response: Saying something like "I'm delivering on everything you've asked, so what I do with my remaining time is my call." Which is true. But I keep imagining how that would land — confrontational, difficult, not a team player. So I default to the smooth version. And then I'm annoyed at myself for it later, which is its own kind of exhausting.
Got the tech lead offer. Accepted on the spot because that's what you do. Texted Noor and she sent about fifteen celebration emojis. Calling my parents tonight.

Same day, later. Parents were thrilled, mum teared up a little. Had a glass of wine with Noor and she kept saying she knew it would happen. Everyone's reaction confirmed that this was the right move, or at least the expected one, and those two things feel like they should be different but tonight they're the same and I'm too tired to untangle it.

I keep coming back to something small. When Martijn told me, my first feeling wasn't excitement. It was relief. Relief that it's done, that I don't have to hold the performance anymore, that I can stop wondering and just be in the role. Maybe excitement comes later. Or maybe relief is enough. I genuinely don't know and I'm going to stop thinking about it and go to bed.

Finally told Andrés he can't keep showing up twenty minutes late to install pieces. I've been letting it slide for weeks because he's good with his hands and the clients like him, but this morning he rolled in while a collector was already waiting and I had to cover for him, again. So I pulled him aside after she left and laid it out: one more time and I'm finding someone else.

He looked genuinely startled, which tells me nobody's ever drawn that line with him. Good. I can't have my space looking disorganized when there's money on the table. Felt solid doing it, too. Not angry, just clear. The gallery is mine and the standard is mine to set.

Nudge: "Did the collector say anything about the wait?"

Response: She didn't mention it directly, but she kept checking her phone while I was making small talk. Ended up buying the smaller piece instead of the one she'd asked about originally. Could be coincidence, could be she lost patience. Either way, that's exactly why I can't let things slide.

Long day finishing the commission for the Restrepo family. Got the last panel mounted and stepped back to look at the whole triptych together. It works. The proportions are right, the color transitions hold. I stood there for a minute just looking at it and felt something click into place, that rare moment when what I made matches what I saw in my head.

Called my mom after. She asked about the gallery rent, whether I'd paid on time. I had, obviously. I always do. She worries, but she doesn't need to — I've had the next three months covered since January. Told her the commission check clears Friday. She sounded relieved, which is enough for now.

Nudge: "What was that click like?"

Met with a potential co-investor for the expansion into the second floor. He kept talking over me, pitching his own vision for the layout. I smiled and nodded through most of it instead of pushing back, and now I'm sitting here irritated at myself.

Walked through the Saturday market in Provenza this morning, not to buy anything but just to look at how other people display their work. The ceramicist on the corner has started using linen backdrops and better lighting — she's learning. A year ago her booth looked like a garage sale. Now people stop.

I do this thing where I watch who commands a space and who just fills it. The ceramicist commands hers now. It reminded me why I fought so hard to get the gallery out of the shared lease arrangement. When I had to negotiate every wall placement with Camilo, nothing felt fully mine. Now every corner of that space reflects a decision I made, and collectors can feel it. That matters more than people think.

Lazy Sunday, which almost never happens. Woke up late, made arepas with the leftover hogao from Friday. Sat on the balcony eating and watching the street below. The neighbor's kid was kicking a ball against the wall again, steady thunk-thunk-thunk rhythm that's almost calming when you're not trying to concentrate.

Started going through my supply invoices because even on a day off I can't leave the numbers alone. Paints are up twelve percent from last year which is absurd, and I'm running through the cadmium yellow faster than usual because of the Restrepo palette. Need to either find a new supplier or adjust my pricing. Probably both. I've been meaning to rework the pricing sheet anyway — the last revision was August and costs have shifted.
