Nothing urgent. The accounts are fine. I just like knowing exactly where the money sits, what's coming in, what's going out. My mother would say I got that from my father, who used to keep a paper ledger for his tire shop even after they had a computer. Different scale, same instinct.

Spent the afternoon finalizing the March exhibition lineup and it felt like orchestrating something, which is the part of this work I love most. Three emerging artists, each one I handpicked. Two of them came to me through referrals from collectors I've built relationships with over years. The third found me through Instagram, sent a portfolio that was raw but had something. I'm taking a risk on her, but a calculated one — I've already soft-committed two buyers who tend to go for that aesthetic.

The thing is, when I curate a show, I'm not just hanging art. I'm deciding whose career gets a platform and whose doesn't. That responsibility sits well with me. I spent years being the one hoping someone would give me a shot. Now I'm on the other side. I've earned that.

Had a good call with Daniela, my accountant, beforehand. March projections are strong, the reserve fund is untouched, insurance renewal is handled. When the financial side is solid, I can actually think about the art. When it's shaky, everything else feels shaky too. I learned that the hard way during the first year after the split with Camilo, when I was burning through savings and couldn't focus on a single canvas.

Nudge: "What was the something in her portfolio?"

Response: Honestly, she reminded me of myself ten years ago. Same raw energy, same refusal to make the work palatable for easy sales. I almost didn't reach out because I don't usually go for sentimental reasons. But then I looked at her pieces again and they're genuinely good. I just didn't want to admit that the recognition was part of why I noticed her.

The co-investor sent a revised proposal with his name listed as "co-director" of the new floor. I didn't agree to that. I wrote back saying we need to clarify terms before anything moves forward, but my hands were shaking when I hit send. Not from fear — from the idea that someone thinks they can just slide into a title I haven't offered.

Nudge: "What were the hands shaking from, then?"

Response: Just adrenaline. It's fine. The email's sent, the boundary is drawn. Moving on.

March show opens Friday. All three artists delivered on time. Walked through the hung pieces this evening and everything is exactly where I placed it. Good feeling, knowing the room is set and the invitations went out to the right people.

Opening night went better than I planned. Sold four pieces in the first two hours, including one from the new artist — the one I took a chance on. A collector from Bogotá asked for my card and said he'd been hearing my name. That sentence alone made the whole month worth it. This is what building a reputation looks like, one show at a time, one handshake at a time.

Called my mom after closing up. She asked how much I made and I told her, which I don't usually do, but I wanted her to hear it. Wanted her to know the gallery isn't some reckless bet anymore. She said "qué bueno, mija" in that tone that means she's genuinely proud but won't say it bigger than that. That's enough. The numbers are good, the reserve is intact, and people are starting to know the name Villalobos in this city's art scene.

Someone on an art forum called my curation "commercially safe." I almost replied but closed the tab instead. My gallery, my choices, my track record. If making a living from art while keeping my lights on and my artists paid is "safe," fine. I'll take it.

Nudge: "What were you going to type?"

Response: Something about how I left a stable partnership to build this alone and nobody who's done that gets to be called safe. But then I realized I'd be arguing with a stranger who's never mounted a show or signed a lease. Not worth it. I know what I've built.

The co-investor agreed to drop the co-director title but now wants approval rights on artist selection for the second floor. Told him I'd think about it. I won't give that up, but I also can't fund the expansion alone and the bank terms are worse. Feels like I'm slowly trading control for capital and I'm not sure where the line is.

Nudge: "Where would the line be?"

Response: I don't know yet. That's the whole problem. I'll figure it out before the next meeting.

Had a terrible shift today and I can't stop going over it. We had a team meeting about the new triage protocols and someone — one of the younger consultants — made a comment about how we should be "more selective" about which patients get palliative referrals because the ward is overstretched. He was basically implying we should deprioritise patients who don't have family pushing for it, which in practice means the homeless patients, the migrants, the people nobody's advocating for. I sat there and I knew exactly what he meant and I said nothing. A few people nodded. Anke looked uncomfortable but she didn't speak up either.

I've been doing this work for thirty years and I just let it slide. I told myself it was because the meeting was already running long and tempers were short, but honestly I think I was just tired. Tired and afraid of being that older nurse who's always making everything political. But those are real people he was talking about, people I've held hands with while they died. Nobody should have to earn the right to a dignified death by having relatives who make enough noise.

I'm angry at myself. I keep rehearsing what I should have said and it's making my stomach hurt. Willem would tell me to let it go and pick my battles, but this feels like exactly the kind of battle I should have picked.

Nudge: "What stopped you in that moment?"

Response: I think I was just exhausted and didn't want to be the difficult one again.

Spent the afternoon at the refugee clinic. There was a Syrian woman, maybe my age, who came in with chronic back pain that's been ignored for months because she keeps getting shuffled between GP practices and nobody follows through. I got her a proper referral to the physiotherapist and explained everything in simple Dutch, and her daughter translated the rest. When she left she squeezed my hand and said something in Arabic I didn't understand but I could tell what it meant.

Those moments remind me why I do what I do. It's not heroic, it's just basic decency — making sure someone gets the care they should have had weeks ago. The system fails these people constantly and most of the time there's nobody there to catch them. I can't fix everything but I can make sure the ones who come through my door don't fall through the cracks.

Walked home along the Maas afterwards and the late afternoon light on the water was beautiful. Felt like myself for the first time all week. Made a simple stamppot for dinner and sat in the garden with a glass of wine and the radio on. Days like this keep me going.

Saturday. Slept in which I almost never do. Made coffee and read the Volkskrant at the kitchen table like I used to when the kids were small, except now the house is quiet and nobody's fighting over the last beschuit.

The paper had a big piece about plastic pollution in the North Sea and it got me going again. They found microplastics in fish at the market in Scheveningen. I remember swimming there as a girl and the water was already dirty then, but at least we weren't eating the pollution. Now it's in everything. I want to feel like individual choices matter — I bring my own bags, I cycle, I sort my recycling obsessively — but these articles make it feel so small against the scale of it. Shell is right there across the river and they're still doing what they've always done.

Went grocery shopping after, argued with myself about buying the organic tomatoes that cost twice as much. Bought them anyway. Had a quiet afternoon fixing the hem on my uniform trousers and listening to a podcast about healthcare in sub-Saharan Africa. Normal day really. Nothing remarkable.

Nudge: "Does that smallness ever make you want to stop trying?"

Response: No. It just makes me angrier at the ones who could actually change things and choose not to.

Long week. Thursday and Friday were double shifts because Pieter called in sick and nobody else could cover. By Friday afternoon I was running on automatic. One of my patients, an elderly man with no family contact, was scheduled for a comfort care review. I should have sat with him properly, gone through everything, made sure he understood all the options. Instead I rushed through it in ten minutes because I had three other patients waiting and the ward was short-staffed.

He deserved better than that. Every patient deserves a proper conversation about their care, especially when there's nobody else asking questions on their behalf. But I chose efficiency because I was stretched thin and it was the easier option. Ticked the box, moved on, felt wrong about it immediately.

The system is designed this way — there are never enough people, never enough time, and eventually everyone starts cutting corners. The corners you cut are always on the patients who won't complain. I know this. I've given talks about this. And I still did it. The gap between what I know is right and what I actually do when I'm exhausted is wider than I'd like to admit.

Nudge: "Would you have rushed if he'd had family there watching?"

Response: Probably not. And that's the worst part.
Colleague made a joke about the refugee patients "costing us all money" at lunch and everyone laughed. I didn't laugh but I didn't say anything either. I'm not their teacher.

Been thinking about this all weekend and I need to write it down. Last Tuesday the ward got offered a bulk supply deal on disposable gloves and gowns from a company I know uses factories with terrible labour conditions in Southeast Asia. I've read the reports, I've seen the articles. The alternative supplier is more expensive and our budget is already squeezed. I signed off on the cheaper option because the ward manager was pushing hard and I didn't want to hold up the order for something nobody else seemed to care about.

It's such a small thing in the grand scheme. Nobody dies because of which glove supplier we use. But it's the principle — I spend my weekends at the refugee clinic working with people who've fled exactly these kinds of conditions, and then on a Tuesday morning I quietly approve the purchase order that keeps those conditions going. The hypocrisy of it sits badly.

I keep telling myself I'm one person and I can't fight every fight. But I also know that's what everyone tells themselves and it's how nothing ever changes. Willem says I take too much on and that it's not my job to fix global supply chains. He's probably right practically but it doesn't make me feel less complicit.

Nudge: "What would it have cost you to push back on Tuesday?"

Response: Probably just an awkward meeting. That's what bothers me.

Can't sleep so I'm writing more. Still stuck on the glove thing from earlier but it's connecting to something bigger now. At the ethics panel meeting last month, there was a discussion about end-of-life care guidelines for undocumented patients. The hospital legal team sent someone who basically said we should follow the strict protocol, which in practice means reduced access for people without valid papers. I pushed back in the meeting — I did speak up that time — but when the vote came I abstained because I didn't want to be the only dissenting voice on record. The motion passed.

I told myself abstaining was a form of protest, that it showed I didn't agree. But really I just didn't want my name attached to a losing vote. That's cowardice dressed up as strategy. These are people's lives and I was protecting my position on the panel.

The pattern is clear when I write it all out like this. I care deeply and I act cautiously and there's a gap between the two that keeps growing. I'm fifty-eight years old and I've built a reputation as someone who cares about these things, but when it costs me something — even something small like an awkward moment or a dissenting vote — I back down. I don't know what to do with that.

Nudge: "When was the last time you didn't back down?"
Quiet Sunday. Cycled to the market, bought too much cheese, came home and pottered around the garden pulling weeds. Nothing on my mind for once.

Today I drove to work instead of cycling because it was raining hard and I was running late. Sat in traffic on the A20 for forty minutes, engine running, watching the Shell refinery chimneys pump out whatever it is they pump out, and I thought about what a fraud I am. I have a sign on my fridge that says "There is no Planet B" and there I was sitting in a diesel car because I didn't want to get wet.

It's not just the driving. I had a coffee from the machine downstairs in a disposable cup because I forgot my thermos. I ordered something from Amazon last week because it was cheaper and I needed it quickly when I could have waited and bought it from the local shop. Each thing on its own is nothing. But they add up and I'm adding them up and I don't like the total.

I talk to my daughter Lotte about these things and she says I'm too hard on myself, that systemic problems need systemic solutions and individual guilt is a distraction. She's probably right intellectually. But I grew up in a generation that was told every action matters, that you vote with your wallet, that if everyone just did their bit. And now I'm not even doing my bit consistently. The convenience keeps winning and I keep noticing and it's exhausting.

Nudge: "Is the guilt actually changing anything?"

Response: Honestly no. It just makes me tired.

Staff meeting today. The new director gave a presentation about "efficiency metrics" and patient throughput targets. During the Q&A, one of the junior nurses — Fatima, she's been here about eight months — asked a very good question about whether these targets would mean less time per patient for the non-Dutch-speaking patients who need interpreters. The director gave a non-answer about "equitable resource allocation" and moved on.

After the meeting, Fatima looked deflated. She came to me in the hallway and said she felt like nobody supported her question. I told her it was a good question and I agreed with her concern. She asked why I hadn't said anything during the meeting. I said something about it being a big room and timing not being right. She just nodded and walked off.

I've been that young nurse asking the hard question in a room full of people who agree privately and stay silent publicly. I know exactly how that feels and I let it happen to someone else. She needed one senior person to back her up and I didn't do it. The timing excuse is nonsense. I just didn't want to be seen challenging the new director in his first week. Politics over principle again. I should have backed her up.

Nudge: "Are you going to talk to Fatima?"

Response: I think I have to. Not just for her. I need to know I'm still capable of following through.
Brought Fatima a coffee this morning and told her I'd raise her question at the next ethics panel. She smiled. Small thing but it felt right.

Finished the last piece for the Jabal Amman gallery order today. Pulled each one out of the kiln myself, wrapped them in tissue, lined them up on the shelf. Four months of work sitting there looking exactly the way I wanted them to look.

Nudge: "What hit you when you saw them all lined up?"

Response: Relief, honestly. That this batch came out right and the gallery check will clear next week.

My cousin Fadi was in town from Dubai and we had dinner at my uncle's place. He kept going on about his new project management role, the relocation package, the company car. My aunt turned to me at one point and said, "You see, Nadia, that is what happens when you follow a plan." I smiled and ate my kibbeh and did not say anything.

The thing is, I do have a plan. I have the gallery contract through October, the workshop income, the private commissions spreadsheet. It just does not look like a plan to people who need a company name on it. I left dinner early and drove home with the windows down and felt fine about it. I did.

Nudge: "Did you actually feel fine?"

Response: No. I kept replaying my aunt's voice the whole drive home. It is not that she is wrong about Fadi doing well — he is. It is that she cannot see what I have built because it does not come with a business card.

Spent most of the morning at the studio doing inventory. Ran the numbers on clay and glaze stock for the next quarter and placed an order with the Turkish supplier. The exchange rate is worse this month, so I bought less than I wanted to. I will need to reorder before October if the gallery pieces go ahead.

Afternoon was a walk-in workshop — six women from a corporate team-building group. They made pinch pots and seemed to enjoy it. Easy money, low creative effort on my part. I have three more of these booked through August, which helps with the monthly numbers.

Back again. Could not stop thinking about what I wrote earlier, about the workshops. I want to be clear — I am not ungrateful. The workshop income is what lets me say no to design freelance gigs I do not want. It is a trade I chose and I would choose it again.

It is just that sometimes the choosing gets heavy. Every month is a little negotiation between what I want to make and what I need to sell. I keep both ledgers and they rarely agree. But they do not have to agree. They just have to coexist.

Had a call with Reem from the Wekalat gallery in Beirut. She wants to include two of my pieces in a group show in November. No artist fee, just exposure and potential sales. I said I would think about it, but what I was really doing was calculating shipping costs from Amman to Beirut in my head while she talked.

I decided yes. The pieces she wants are ones I made for myself, not on commission. Letting them go to a show where someone might actually connect with them — that matters more to me than the logistics headache. I will figure out the shipping.

Nudge: "Which pieces did she pick?"

Signed a six-month lease renewal on the studio today. Landlord kept it at the same rate, which almost never happens here. Called my mother afterward and she said, "Good, at least something is fixed."

Nudge: "What did she mean by 'at least'?"

One of the corporate workshop pots cracked in the kiln. Not a client piece, just a leftover someone forgot. But I spent twenty minutes staring at it anyway because the glaze had done something unexpected on the broken edge — this deep copper-green that I have been trying to get for months on my own work and it just showed up on a throwaway pot by accident.

I cannot explain why that made me so frustrated. All the careful testing, the temperature logs, the glaze ratios written out in notebooks. And the thing I have been chasing appears on a piece that no one will ever claim. I scraped the glaze residue off and saved it in a jar. I will try to figure out what happened. That is all I can do.

Shipped the two pieces for the Beirut show today. Wrapped them myself in three layers — bubble wrap, then foam, then a wooden crate I built from scrap lumber in the back of the studio. The shipping guy looked at me like I was overdoing it, but those vessels took me five weeks each and I am not trusting them to someone else's packing job.

After they left, the studio felt different. Emptier, obviously, but also lighter. I have been holding onto those pieces since April, moving them from shelf to shelf, never quite sure what they were for. Now they have a destination and I can stop hovering. Reem sent a photo of the gallery space where they will be placed — a corner with natural light from a high window. They will look right there.

Spent the rest of the afternoon mixing a new glaze batch based on the copper-green residue from last week. I adjusted the iron oxide ratio and ran a small test tile. Will not know until tomorrow. The kiln schedule is full this week with workshop pieces, so I wedged my test into the last corner of the bottom shelf where the temperature runs slightly cooler. We will see.

The test tile came out close. Not exact — the copper-green is more muted than the accident, and it does not have that sharp metallic edge I saw on the broken pot. But it is in the neighborhood. I mixed a second batch with slightly more copper carbonate and will fire again Thursday.

My mother called this morning. She sounded tired. She asked about money, which she does when she is worried about her own but does not want to say so. I told her the gallery payment came through and that the workshop bookings are steady through September. She seemed relieved, or at least she stopped asking follow-up questions. I wired her some money through CliQ after we hung up — not a lot, but enough that she will not have to ask again for a while.

Sometimes I think the reason I track every fils so carefully is not really about the studio at all. It is about being able to pick up the phone and say "everything is fine" and have it be true. Or close enough to true. If my numbers are solid, I can carry more than just my own weight, and that is the part that lets me sleep at night.

Nudge: "When did you start carrying that weight?"

Response: When my father died and the bills started arriving at her door. I was twenty-six. I remember sitting at the kitchen table in Beirut doing the math and thinking, I need to always be able to do this math, no matter what.

Workshop cancelled last minute — the group rescheduled to next month. That is 400 dinars I was counting on for September's clay order. I sat in the studio for an hour doing nothing, just staring at the schedule board and rearranging numbers in my head.

Nudge: "What are you going to do?"

Response: I will figure it out. I always do.

September already. Pulled up the spreadsheet this morning and went through everything — studio rent, clay orders, kiln fuel, the shipping costs from the Beirut pieces. After the cancelled workshop, the buffer is thinner than I like. Not dangerous, but I can feel the margin. I have two options: pick up a small freelance design job to bridge the gap, or book an extra workshop weekend and push the clay order back two weeks.

I chose the workshop. Spent an hour writing a post for the studio's page, offering a weekend intensive for beginners. It is more of the same work I was just complaining about, but at least it is mine — my studio, my schedule, my terms. The freelance route would have paid better but I would have spent a week taking direction from someone else's brief, and I left that behind for a reason.

Reem messaged that the Beirut show opens next Friday. She said there has been some early interest in the vessels. I read her message three times, then put the phone down and went back to wedging clay. I do not want to get ahead of myself. If they sell, good — it changes the math. If they do not, the math still works, just tighter. Either way, I keep making the work.

Mummyji made a comment at lunch about the indigo stains on my fingernails. She didn't say anything directly, just that thing she does where she looks at my hands and then looks at the dal and raises her eyebrows. Ravi was right there and said nothing. I know he thinks the dyeing thing is fine, he's told me so, but he won't say it in front of her. So I'm the one who looks like I'm neglecting things.

The thing is, the kitchen was spotless. Lunch was on time. Arjun's tiffin was packed before school. I don't know what more she wants from me. Or maybe I do know and I just don't want to give it, which is this complete surrender of any time that's mine. Like the house should swallow me whole and I should be grateful for it.

I scrubbed the indigo off after lunch. Took ten minutes with lemon and baking soda. I shouldn't have had to do that. But I did it anyway and I'm not sure what that says about me.

Nudge: "Why did you scrub them off?"

Response: Because it was easier than sitting through another silent dinner with her staring at my hands. I pick my fights and that one wasn't worth it. But it felt like erasing something.

Arjun's parent-teacher meeting today. The class teacher mentioned he draws during free period instead of playing cricket with the other boys and asked if everything is okay at home. I wanted to say, yes, everything is fine, my son likes drawing, why is that a problem? But I smiled and nodded and said I'd encourage him to be more social.

Came home and told Ravi about it and he said maybe Arjun should join a sport, that it would be good for him. And I thought, here we go. Everyone has an opinion about what the boy should be doing except the boy himself.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying that to the teacher?"

Response: I don't know. You just don't, do you? Not in that setting.

Made aloo paratha for breakfast the way Appa used to, with extra ajwain in the dough. The smell filled the whole kitchen and for a second I was back in our old house in Mysore, watching him roll them out, flour everywhere.

Spent the whole morning at the Diwali mela helping set up the community puja. I did the rangoli at the entrance the way my grandmother taught me, rice flour with turmeric borders, none of the acrylic paint shortcuts the younger crowd uses. Three women came up and asked me to show them how. Felt good.

Later, back home, I worked on a new indigo resist pattern. Nobody was around to comment on it. Ravi had taken Arjun and Vihaan to the cricket match. Two hours of silence and my hands doing what they wanted to do. I didn't even check my phone.

Nudge: "Which part of the day stuck with you more?"

Mummyji wants me to start teaching Vihaan the Vishnu Sahasranama. He's seven. She says she learned it by six. I said I'd start next week, which I will, because it's important, Appa made sure I knew it by heart and I'm glad he did. But there's this edge to the way she asks, like she's testing whether I'm doing enough.

Ravi's cousin Priya called in the evening. She started a small catering business last year and wanted to know if I'd be interested in supplying hand-dyed table linens. I said I'd think about it. Didn't mention it to anyone at home yet.

Nudge: "Are you going to tell Ravi about Priya's offer?"

Sat down this morning to teach Vihaan the first twenty verses. He's quicker than I expected. He kept asking what the words meant and I found myself explaining them properly, not just the rote way I was taught but actually talking about what each name describes. He asked why Vishnu has so many names and I said because one name is never enough for something that big. He seemed satisfied with that.

It reminded me of sitting with Appa in the puja room, early mornings, the smell of camphor and sandalwood paste. He was patient. He never rushed me or made it feel like a chore. I want to give Vihaan that same feeling, not the pressure of getting it right, but the warmth of sitting together with something that matters. The sense that this is ours, this thread that connects us backward through generations, something nobody can take away or schedule over.

Mummyji passed by the room and listened for a while and actually smiled. She didn't say anything, didn't correct his pronunciation or mine. She just stood there and smiled. That was enough for today.

Spent the afternoon cataloguing my dye samples. I have fourteen shades of indigo now, ranging from almost-grey to a deep navy that I got by double-dipping with a fermentation vat I built from a recipe in an old Kalamkari manual. Labelled each one with the mordant used, the dip count, the fabric type. Cotton takes differently than silk, and I'm getting more consistent results with the cotton muslin I sourced from a weaver in Solapur.

Priya messaged again about the linens. She has a wedding order for February. Twelve table runners, twenty napkins. The money would be decent. I told her I need to think about scale. My terrace setup can handle maybe four meters of fabric at a time. I'd need to figure out drying space and timing around the kids' schedules.

Vihaan came up to the terrace after his homework and asked if he could dip his hand in the vat. I let him. His palm came out blue and he ran downstairs to show everyone. Mummyji was not pleased.

Nudge: "So are you doing it?"

Response: Haven't decided. Need to work out the logistics first.

Told Ravi about Priya's proposal over chai after the boys went to bed. He was more supportive than I expected. He said if I wanted to try it, he'd help me figure out the money side. He even suggested converting part of the back store room into a workspace. I hadn't thought about that.

What surprised me was my own hesitation. He was offering exactly what I thought I wanted, backing, space, permission. But when it was right there in front of me I felt this pull backward, like, who am I to run a business? My mother didn't. Mummyji didn't. All the women I grew up watching ran homes, not enterprises. And I love running this home. That's the part that gets lost when people hear I want to do something else. I don't want to leave, I want to also.

Nudge: "What does 'also' actually look like day to day?"

Response: Mornings with the boys, school drop-off, puja, cooking. Afternoons when they're in school or doing homework, that's my window. Three, maybe four hours. It's not a lot but it's mine. I just need people to stop treating those hours like leftover time that belongs to the house.

Mummyji brought up the store room idea at dinner, in front of Ravi's parents who were visiting. She said something like, "Meera is thinking of opening a factory in the house." She was smiling but it was the wrong kind of smile. Ravi's father asked what she was talking about and I had to explain the dyeing and the linen idea in front of everyone while trying not to sound like I was defending myself.

Ravi stepped in eventually and said it was just a small project. The word "small" stuck. I know he was trying to help but it felt like he was shrinking it to make it palatable. I didn't say anything after that. Finished dinner. Cleared the plates. Went upstairs.

Can't sleep. Ravi's parents left after dinner and everyone is being normal now but I keep replaying the table conversation. The worst part isn't what Mummyji said, it's that nobody asked me a real question about it. Nobody said, what kind of fabric, what's the process, how did you learn. They only wanted to know if it would interfere with anything.

I keep thinking about Appa's workshop in Mysore. He repaired watches. Tiny, patient, precise work. Nobody ever questioned whether he should be doing it. It was just what he did. I want that. Not the permission, not the approval. Just the quiet assumption that of course I do this, it's mine.

Nudge: "What would Appa say about the indigo?"

Took the boys to the Ganesh temple this morning for the monthly abhishekam. Vihaan recited the first twenty verses of the Sahasranama on the auto ride there, unprompted. I didn't even know he'd been practising on his own. The priest noticed and said something to me about keeping these things alive. I felt proud but also this strange defensiveness, like I needed to prove I wasn't only about the dyeing, that I still carry all of this too.

Why do I feel like I have to choose? Like every hour I spend with the indigo is an hour stolen from something else. Nobody told Ravi he has to choose between his consulting work and being a father. It's just assumed he can be both.

Nudge: "Who's actually making you choose?"

Called Priya back tonight and said yes to the February order. Twelve runners, twenty napkins, indigo with a kalamkari-inspired border block I've been working on. She sounded happy. I felt terrified and then calm, in that order, like jumping into cold water.

Haven't told Mummyji yet. Told Ravi, who said congratulations and then asked if I needed help setting up the store room this weekend. I said yes. Vihaan overheard and asked if he could help with the blue. I said yes to that too. I don't know if I'm doing the right thing. But I know I'm doing my thing, and right now that has to be enough.

Pieter talked right over me in the steering committee today, mid-sentence, and presented the container routing proposal like it was his. I just sat there. Too tired to fight it, too angry to let it go, and now it's midnight and I'm still turning it over.

Nudge: "What stopped you from calling it out right there?"

Ran the quarterly review myself today, no slides from anyone else, just my numbers, my narrative. The room was quiet in that good way where people are actually listening. Felt like the division is finally mine in the way I wanted it to be.

Needed sign-off from Legal on the new supplier contract. Straightforward, or so I thought. Sent it Thursday, followed up Friday morning, got told Anke was "reviewing priorities" and would get to it when she could. Meanwhile the supplier's deadline is Tuesday and I'm the one who'll look like I dropped the ball.

It's not even about the contract, really. It's that I had to sit there refreshing my inbox like some junior waiting for permission. I run a forty-three person division and I can't move a standard supplier agreement through because someone in a different department decides the timeline. I almost went to the CEO directly but thought better of it. Probably should have.

Nudge: "Why did you hold back from going to the CEO?"
Routine day. Back-to-back calls, budget reconciliation, nothing unusual. Grabbed a broodje kroket from the canteen and ate it at my desk between meetings.

Monday. Staff meeting went long because Thomas wanted to discuss the new reporting structure again. I'd already decided on it two weeks ago and communicated it clearly, but apparently some people need to re-litigate everything. Let them talk for ten minutes, then moved on.

Lotte asked how work was over dinner. I said fine. She gave me that look, the one that means she knows I'm not actually answering. I wasn't trying to be evasive, I just genuinely didn't have the energy to translate the day into something she'd find interesting. Watched the football afterward, fell asleep on the couch again.

Had a clear plan for the fleet optimization rollout. Presented it in the ops meeting. Sandra pushed back hard, said we should phase it differently, go region by region instead of all at once. Others started nodding along with her, and I could feel the room shifting.

I ended up agreeing to her phased approach. It's not wrong, technically. But it wasn't my call anymore, and everyone in that room saw it happen. I keep telling myself it doesn't matter how we get there as long as we get there. Not sure I believe that.

Nudge: "What exactly changed when the room shifted to Sandra's side?"

Response: It wasn't about the plan. It was that they looked at her like she was in charge of the decision.

Sunday but I can't stop thinking about Friday. New hire, Jeroen, fresh from his MBA, has been here eight weeks. In the project kickoff he interrupted me to "build on" my point, then spent five minutes explaining container throughput metrics to me like I haven't been running this operation for a decade. The room just let it happen. Nobody stepped in.

What gets me is that I know this kid's type. He's positioning. He's already had coffee with the CEO twice, which I only know because I saw it in the calendar. And I just sat there and let him reframe my own analysis back to me. I should have shut it down. I should have said, "Jeroen, I've been doing this since before you started your bachelor's." But I didn't. I smiled and nodded like it was a collaborative discussion.

I keep thinking about what my father would say. He ran his own business for thirty years and nobody ever talked over him because he made sure of it from day one. I don't know when I started letting people do this to me. Maybe it's the restructure, maybe I'm spread too thin to fight every battle. But it feels like something I'm losing and I can't quite name what it is.

Nudge: "What is it you think you're losing?"

Response: The room's assumption that I'm the one who decides.
Pieter did it again in the client call. Spoke over me, redirected the conversation to his numbers. I corrected him this time but it came out sharper than I meant and the client noticed.

Quiet Sunday, Lotte took the girls to her mother's in Leiden. House to myself. Made coffee the slow way, French press, sat in the kitchen looking at the garden. The hedge needs cutting again. Thought about calling my brother but didn't, we'd just end up talking about the house in Zeeland that neither of us wants to deal with.

Spent an hour going through old emails, not for work reasons, just sorting. Found some from when I first got promoted to division head. I was so careful in those early messages, checking every word. Now I fire things off without thinking. Not sure if that's confidence or just not caring as much.

Had a one-on-one with Lisa from my team today. She's been struggling with the new reporting system and came in looking like she expected to be reprimanded. I didn't do that. Just walked through it with her, showed her the shortcuts I use, told her it took me three months to stop hating the interface too. She actually laughed.

Small thing, but it reminded me of something. When people come to me for direction and I give it clearly, no games, no politics, just here's how we do this — that's when I feel most like myself. Not the boardroom stuff, not the jockeying. Just someone who knows what they're doing, passing it on. Drove home in a decent mood for once.

Board prep meeting today. The CFO asked me to "walk him through" my division's numbers as if I hadn't already submitted a forty-page deck last week. He does this to me every quarter. He doesn't do it to Pieter, doesn't do it to Sandra. Just me. Like I need to audition for the right to present my own results.

I complied, obviously. Went through it line by line, smiled, answered his follow-ups. Drove home and sat in the car in the garage for ten minutes before going inside. Lotte asked if I was okay and I said yes. I wasn't really.

Nudge: "What were you thinking about in those ten minutes in the car?"

Response: Just decompressing. Long day.
End of Q1. Numbers look solid, team's holding together, nothing on fire. Booked a table at De Kas for Lotte's birthday next week. Normal Monday, which at this point I'll take.

The faculty review came back today and my department scored highest on the new pedagogical alignment rubric. Twenty-eight years and it still lands when the numbers confirm what you feel in the classroom. I printed the summary and put it in the drawer with the others.

Ran into Katja on the way out, one of the newer teachers I meet on Mondays. She was upset about a parent complaint and I stayed an extra forty minutes walking her through how to frame the follow-up email. Got home late again but Brigitte had already eaten. Didn't say anything about it. I think she's stopped expecting dinner together on school days.

Nudge: "What did Brigitte not saying anything feel like?"

Response: Honestly, worse than if she had complained. When she used to get frustrated I could at least argue I was doing something that mattered. The silence feels like she's decided it's not worth the effort. And I don't know what to do with that because the work genuinely does matter to me, but so does she. I just can't seem to make the hours stretch.

Markus got the state teaching prize nomination. Markus. He's been at the school nine years and I recommended him for the department two years ago because I saw potential. Which is fine, good for him, I even wrote a reference. But I keep circling back to the fact that nobody thought to put my name forward and I've been building this department since before he finished university.

Maybe it's that the committee wants fresh faces. Or maybe my work has become furniture, something everyone relies on but nobody actually sees. I spent an hour tonight reorganising the spreadsheet of student outcomes to see if I'm slipping. I'm not. The numbers are solid. But the numbers don't seem to matter to whoever fills out the nomination form.

Nudge: "You recommended him and he got the nomination. Where does that leave you?"

Monday coffee with Katja, Leon, and Simone. Katja brought a lesson plan she'd redesigned using a framework I introduced two years ago and it was genuinely good. I told her so. Leon asked if I'd review his assessment rubric by Wednesday. Simone needed help navigating a difficult student's parents. I said yes to both without thinking.

Walking home I realised I hadn't touched my own conference paper all week. The submission deadline is in three weeks and I've been telling myself I'd carve out evenings for it. Instead the evenings go to other people's problems. I don't resent them for asking. I resent myself for not being able to say not this week.

Parent evening ran until nine. One father spent twenty minutes questioning why his son got a B+ instead of an A on the Cold War unit and I had to sit there with my folder of rubrics defending a grade I know is fair. This is the part of the job that drains me. Not the teaching, the performing.

Got home and Brigitte asked if I'd remembered to call my sister for her birthday. I hadn't. Sat on the couch for ten minutes not moving before I picked up the phone. Hilde sounded fine but I could tell she noticed I was going through the motions. Another thing I let slide because the school day swallowed everything.

Sunday. Actually managed the whole afternoon without opening the laptop. Brigitte and I drove out to the Eifel, walked a trail we used to do when the kids were small. She pointed out the bench where Thomas fell asleep once, years ago, and we both laughed. It felt like coming back to something.

On the drive home I told her about the conference paper and how behind I am. She said maybe I should let the Monday coffees go for a few weeks. I know she's right in a practical sense. But those three depend on me right now, especially Katja, and I think what I give them matters more than another line on my CV. Or maybe I just tell myself that because it's easier than sitting alone with a half-finished draft.

Nudge: "What would happen if you told them you needed a few weeks?"

Markus won the prize. Everyone clapped at the staff meeting and I clapped too. My hands felt mechanical.

Nudge: "What were you thinking while you clapped?"

Response: That the cake they brought was dry. No, I don't know. It's done, he earned it, I should move on.
Spent the evening reviewing Leon's rubric instead of the conference paper again. It's becoming a pattern I can see clearly but apparently not interrupt.

Submitted a request to attend the European History Educators Conference in June. The head approved it immediately, said I'm long overdue for professional development. That felt decent. It's been four years since my last conference.

Called Hilde properly this time, no agenda, just to talk. She told me about her garden renovation and I actually listened instead of mentally drafting lesson plans. She said I sounded different, more present. I'll take that.

Second thought today. After calling Hilde I sat with my conference paper draft and got two decent pages written. The argument is about how we teach democratic participation as abstract principle rather than practiced skill, and once I started pulling examples from my own classroom it flowed better than I expected.

Brigitte came in with tea around ten and read over my shoulder for a minute. She said it sounded like me at my best, which is the kind of thing she doesn't say often enough for me to take it lightly. I saved the file and actually closed the laptop before midnight. Small accomplishment but I'll count it.

Nudge: "What made tonight different from the other evenings?"

Monday coffee again. This time I told the three of them I'd need to scale back for the next two weeks because of the paper deadline. Katja immediately said of course, Leon nodded, Simone looked a bit lost but said she understood. Nobody collapsed. Nobody resented it, at least not visibly.

Walking home I felt lighter than I have in weeks but also oddly guilty, as though I'd broken an unspoken contract. Brigitte would say that contract only exists in my head. She'd probably be right. I sat down with the draft again after dinner and got another page done. The structure is taking shape.

Nudge: "Where does that unspoken contract come from?"

Response: My father, probably. He was a teacher too, and his door was always open. Students, colleagues, neighbours. I grew up believing that being good at what you do means being available to everyone who needs you. The problem is you can spend a whole career being available and still feel like you haven't done the thing you were actually capable of. I don't want to retire with a drawer full of other people's thank-you cards and an empty shelf where my own work should have been.

Brigitte said something last night that I keep turning over. We were in the kitchen and she mentioned, casually, that she'd stopped inviting the Fischers for dinner because I always cancel or sit there half-present checking my phone for student emails. I started to argue but she cut me off and said she wasn't angry, just adjusting. That word, adjusting, hit harder than any argument.

I've been telling myself the work matters, and it does. The department results prove it, the younger teachers tell me constantly how much the Monday sessions help. But Brigitte adjusting means she's already factored in my absence as the default. My sister noticed the same thing on the phone last week. I don't think I'm neglecting them on purpose. It's more that every time I choose between something with a measurable outcome and something that just asks me to be present, I pick the one I can point to later and say, see, this counted.

I don't know when being present stopped counting for me. Or if it ever did count and I just never built the habit of valuing it properly. The conference paper is going well now and I should feel satisfied. But Brigitte adjusting is sitting in my chest like a stone and I can't spreadsheet my way out of it.

Vikram got promoted to Staff Engineer today. Everyone's congratulating him on Slack, and yes, fine, he deserved it. But let's be honest—he's been here six months longer than me and his scope was smaller until Q2. I'm not bitter. I'm just aware of where I stand. My manager keeps telling me my time will come, but I've heard that before at my last company. You have to make your time come.

Called Appa tonight. He asked about work, and I told him everything was progressing well. No point worrying him with the details. He mentioned the temple renovation fund and I sent extra this month. These things matter. You take care of your family, you do what's expected—that's not a burden, that's just how it should be.

Nudge: "What would 'making your time come' actually look like?"

Response: Visibility. I need to be leading cross-functional initiatives, not just delivering my own sprint work. I've already started attending product roadmap meetings even when I'm not required. When the reorg happens in Q4, I want my name to come up naturally for the platform team lead role.
Priya asked when I'm taking her to meet my parents. I said after Diwali, maybe. We both know I'm stalling but she didn't push.

Three nights of on-call rotations. The junior devs can't seem to handle basic incident triage without escalating to me. I don't mind being the one they depend on, but I need sleep.

Nudge: "Is there a difference between being needed and being indispensable?"

Response: Indispensable means they can't function without you. Needed just means they prefer having you around. I'd rather be indispensable, even if it costs me sleep. Nobody promotes the guy who's easily replaced.

Quarterly review feedback came in. My manager said I have strong technical skills but need to demonstrate more "executive presence." I asked what that meant specifically. She said it's about how I communicate upward—being more concise in leadership meetings, framing problems in terms of business impact rather than technical detail.

I've always believed that doing excellent work should speak for itself. That's what my father taught me—results matter, not showmanship. But apparently that's not how it works here. The people who get ahead are the ones who know how to package themselves.

Spent the evening reading articles about executive communication. Downloaded a book on stakeholder management. I don't like that I have to play this game, but I've never failed at something I committed to learning. If this is what they want, I'll figure it out.

What frustrates me is that nobody told me the rules changed. At IIT, you studied hard, you got the marks, you got the placement. Clear metrics. Here it's all subjective—"presence," "influence," "visibility." How do you measure that? How do you know if you're winning?

Nudge: "What would it mean if excellent work alone was never enough?"

Response: Then everything I was taught about how success works would be wrong. My father worked for the same company for thirty years and retired as a senior manager—never played politics, just delivered results. But maybe that's why he stayed senior manager. Maybe there was a ceiling I didn't see. I don't want that ceiling.

Presented the Q3 roadmap to the leadership team today. Kept it to seven slides, used business metrics instead of technical specs. VP nodded twice. Small win.

Nudge: "How did it feel to communicate that way?"

Priya's friends are getting engaged left and right. She showed me photos from another mehendi last night and I could feel her watching for my reaction. I said the bride looked happy. That's not the response she wanted.

It's not that I don't want to marry her. I do. But there's an order to these things. My parents expect me to be settled—flat, savings, stable position—before I take that step. Amma specifically said not to repeat what her brother did, rushing into marriage without foundation and then struggling for years. I'm building the foundation. Priya should understand that this is for us, not against us.

My cousin Karthik called to announce he's getting married. He's two years younger than me, works at an MNC doing standard IT support, lives in a rented flat in Coimbatore. And yet he's the one Amma spent twenty minutes congratulating on the phone tonight. "Such good news, beta! When will we hear from you?"

I'm the one who sends money home. I'm the one who got into IIT, who moved to Bangalore for better opportunities, who's actually building something. Karthik posts cricket memes and calls it a day. But because he's checking the marriage box, suddenly he's the responsible one?

I'm not jealous of Karthik. His ceiling is that MNC job until retirement. I want more. But I can't help feeling like the family scoreboard doesn't track the things I'm actually achieving. It's all marriage, children, temple visits. The professional stuff—they're proud, sure, but they don't understand it. To them it's just "computer work."

What if I never make them understand? What if I keep climbing and the only thing they see is the boxes I haven't checked?

Nudge: "Whose approval matters more to you right now?"

The platform team lead role opened up officially. My manager submitted my name. I've been preparing all week—reviewing my contributions, documenting the initiatives I've led, practicing how to frame my scope expansion.

Interview is next Thursday. Five panelists including the CTO. I've never presented to him before. My manager said he values people who "think like owners"—whatever that means. I'm guessing it means speaking about company strategy as if you have personal stake in it. I can do that.

Called Priya to tell her. She was supportive but also asked if this meant more late nights. I said probably yes, at least initially. She went quiet. I told her once I get this role, things will stabilize. She said I said that about the senior engineer promotion too. She's not wrong, but what does she expect? This is how you build something. You sacrifice now for position later.

Appa would understand. He always said the early years are for investment, the later years for enjoying the returns. I'm still investing.

Interview went well, I think. CTO asked how I'd handle a direct report who consistently missed deadlines. I said I'd have a direct conversation about expectations and consequences. He seemed to like that.

Nudge: "What's making you unsure about how it went?"

Response: It's not uncertainty, it's just being realistic. You never know what other candidates said or what factors matter to them. I did my part. Now I wait.

Got the role. Platform Team Lead. Officially starts August 15th. Vikram sent me a congratulatory message that was probably through gritted teeth. Doesn't matter. I'm ahead now.

Manager said the CTO specifically mentioned my clarity about accountability. Direct conversations about expectations and consequences—turns out that's exactly his management philosophy. Lucky guess, or maybe I just think the way leaders do. Either way, I'm in.

Nudge: "What does being 'ahead' give you?"

Called home to share the news. Appa congratulated me, asked what the new title means in terms of responsibilities. Explained about headcount, budget ownership, technical direction. He said it sounds like a lot of work. Then asked if this means I'll be too busy to come for Pongal.

I said of course I'll come for Pongal. I always come for Pongal.

Amma asked about Priya. I said we're doing well. She said Karthik's wedding is in November and the family will be asking questions. I said I know. She said maybe Priya could come for the wedding, meet everyone. I said I'd think about it.

After the call, I sat with this feeling I couldn't name. I achieved exactly what I set out to achieve six months ago. Platform Team Lead before 32. Higher salary, direct reports, visibility to the C-suite. But the call home felt like two different conversations—me trying to share a professional milestone, them trying to pull me back to the family timeline.

Maybe both things can be true. Maybe I can lead the platform team and also bring Priya to Karthik's wedding and also send money home and also make Pongal. But it feels like I'm running two races on parallel tracks, and I'm not sure if either one knows the other exists.

Nudge: "What would it look like if both tracks knew about each other?"

Response: I'd have to tell my parents what the promotion actually cost—the late nights, the stress, why I haven't proposed yet. And I'd have to tell Priya that family expectations aren't just background noise to me, they're load-bearing. I don't know if anyone wants that level of honesty. Maybe it's easier to keep the tracks separate.

I had a proper meltdown today, and I'm not even sorry about it. My mother-in-law came over for what was supposed to be a "quick visit" and ended up rearranging half my kitchen because apparently I was storing the spices wrong. Wrong! As if there's a universally correct way to store paprika. I didn't say anything at first, just watched her shuffle things around with that satisfied look on her face, and then something snapped. I told her, very calmly, that I'd prefer she asks before moving things in my home. She looked at me like I'd slapped her.

Erik called later and said she was upset, that I'd embarrassed her. Maybe I did. But this is my space. I've set it up the way that works for me, for Astrid, for our little morning routine where she "helps" me make breakfast by pointing at ingredients. It's taken me months to figure out a system that doesn't make me feel like I'm drowning in domestic chaos. And someone just walked in and decided she knew better.

I know she meant well. I know that. But meaning well isn't the same as respecting that I've built something here on purpose. Not by accident. On purpose.

Nudge: "What specifically about your system makes mornings work?"

Spent the whole morning on a commission that I probably should have turned down. A client wanted a custom piece—a mountain landscape in my style—but then kept sending "small suggestions" that were basically a different style entirely. Softer edges, she said. More realistic trees. At some point I had to ask myself: is this even my work anymore, or am I just a hand executing someone else's vision?

I did finish it, and she loved it. Paid extra, even. But sitting here now, looking at the file, I feel hollow about it. It's not bad. It's just not mine. And that distinction matters to me more than I sometimes admit.

There's a practical voice in my head that says income is income, especially since the shop has been slow this month. But there's another voice—louder, if I'm honest—that asks what the point is if I'm not making the things I actually want to make. I could have spent those hours on my own collection instead. I chose not to. For money. And now I'm sitting with that choice.

I don't regret it exactly. I just notice how it sits differently than the pieces I create for myself.

Nudge: "What would you have drawn for yourself those hours?"

Response: I've had this image in my head for weeks—a single figure standing at the edge of a frozen lake, back to the viewer, and the ice is cracking but in a pattern that looks almost like tree roots spreading. It's about choosing to step forward even when the ground feels unstable. I've sketched it twice but haven't committed to the full piece yet. That's what I would have worked on.

Astrid started saying "no" to everything today and honestly, same. I get it kid. Sometimes every option feels like the wrong one.

Nudge: "Which options?"

Response: Oh, nothing specific really. Just one of those days where even deciding what to make for dinner feels like too much. Probably just tired.

Erik brought up the idea of Astrid starting daycare two days a week. He framed it as giving me more time for the shop, which sounds supportive, but I could hear the subtext loud and clear: he thinks I'm not managing well. Or that I'm wasting time. Or both.

And maybe I am struggling some days. Maybe there are afternoons where Astrid naps and I just sit on the couch scrolling instead of working on prints. But that's my choice to make. I'm the one here every day. I know what I need. Some days I need to create. Some days I need to rest. And I don't need someone who's at an office until 7pm deciding what my schedule should look like.

I know daycare isn't a punishment. Plenty of parents do it, and their kids are fine, great even. It's not about whether daycare is good or bad. It's about this being my decision, about my time, about my daughter—and it wasn't offered as an option. It was presented as a solution to a problem I didn't say I had.

I told him I'd think about it. But I'm not sure what I'm supposed to be thinking about. Whether to accept having my days restructured for me?

Nudge: "What would need to be different for daycare to feel like your idea?"

Later the same day now, after Erik and I talked again. Astrid's finally asleep. I'm on the couch with a glass of wine that I'm too tired to actually drink.

We didn't fight, exactly. He backed off the daycare thing, said he just wanted to help. And I believe him. But I still feel weird. Like I have to defend choices I make in my own home. Like staying home with my daughter and running my shop is something that requires justification.

My mother worked full-time, same as my dad. They split everything 50/50, very modern, very practical. When I told them I was going to stay home after Astrid was born, they didn't say it was wrong, but they didn't say it was right either. Just... nothing. And Erik's family has a stay-at-home mom who followed rules and schedules I can't even fathom. Neither model fits me. I'm not building my life based on their blueprints.

I'm so tired of feeling like I'm doing this wrong no matter which direction I lean. I'm doing it my way. That's supposed to be enough.

Nudge: "What does your way actually look like tomorrow?"

Response: Tomorrow I'll wake up at 5:30 before Astrid, make coffee, and spend an hour on the frozen lake piece before she's up. Then we'll do breakfast together—she helps me pick which fruit to cut. After lunch when she naps, I'll work on shop orders or answer emails, depending on what feels right in that moment. Maybe I'll take a walk with her in the late afternoon to the park she likes. Erik comes home, I hand off, and the evening is mine. That's it. That's my way. Flexible but intentional.

Sold three prints today from my original collection, not commissions. Actual things I made because I wanted to make them. It's not life-changing money but it's proof that what I create on my own terms has value to someone else too.

Astrid poured yogurt on the cat today. I should have been faster but honestly I was sketching in my head and didn't notice she'd climbed onto the counter. We're all learning I guess.

Nudge: "What are you learning?"

Response: That I can't always be in two places at once. That some moments require my full presence and others can bend a little. That the cat will forgive us. That my brain doesn't shut off just because I'm watching a toddler, and maybe that's okay—maybe even necessary for my sanity.

I got an email today from my old agency in Amsterdam. They're doing a freelance project and apparently thought of me. Just a small illustration job, a few days of work, good pay. The catch is I'd need to come into the office for meetings, which means finding childcare for Astrid those days.

I've been staring at the email for three hours. Part of me wants to jump at it—prove that I'm still a real designer, that staying home hasn't erased my skills. Another part feels sick at the thought of slotting back into someone else's schedule, their briefs, their revisions cycle. And then there's the practical part doing math on whether the pay is even worth the hassle of arranging care.

What's strange is how guilty I feel about even considering it. Like wanting something outside of motherhood and my little shop is some kind of betrayal. But also guilty about not wanting it enough. Because shouldn't I be excited? Shouldn't a part of me miss that world?

I don't know what I want. That's the honest answer. I don't know what I want and I hate not knowing.

Nudge: "If you knew no one would judge either choice, which way do you lean?"

Response: Honestly? I lean toward saying no. Not because I'm scared or because it's too hard, but because the thought of sitting in their office making small talk about clients and deadlines makes me feel heavy. I left that for a reason. I don't miss it. I just sometimes miss being seen as competent by people who aren't related to me.

I said no to the Amsterdam job. Wrote the email this morning, polite but clear. And then I finished the frozen lake piece. Both in the same hour, which feels meaningful somehow.

Looking at the finished illustration, I'm struck by how much of me is in it. The figure on the ice—that's not some generic person, it's me, the version of me that's still learning to trust her own footing. The cracks spreading like roots—that's about how breaking apart can also be growing. I don't think anyone buying it will see all that. But I know, and that's what makes it feel real.

I've been thinking about what makes work meaningful to me. It's not the money, though money is necessary. It's not even external recognition, though that's nice. It's whether I can look at something and see my own choices reflected back. When I was at the agency, every project was filtered through client needs, brand guidelines, stakeholder feedback. The work was often beautiful. But it wasn't mine. And that matters to me in a way I've stopped apologizing for.

Astrid pointed at the print today and said "lady cold." Accurate assessment, kid.

Nudge: "What would it look like to fully trust your footing?"

Response: I think it would look like not second-guessing myself when Erik or my parents or anyone else offers an alternative path. Not because their ideas are bad, but because I'd be so secure in mine that I could hear them out without feeling threatened. Right now I still get defensive. Fully trusting would mean being curious instead—hearing what they suggest and knowing I can take it or leave it without my whole sense of direction wobbling.

Listed the frozen lake piece on the shop this morning. Set the price higher than usual because it took more out of me and I want that reflected. Someone already messaged asking if I do bulk orders for offices. I said no.

Also enrolled Astrid in a parent-toddler art class on Thursday mornings. It was my idea, on my timeline. That distinction matters. She'll get to smash paint around with other kids and I'll get to see if I can stand being around other parents for two hours.

The frozen lake piece sold. To a woman in Berlin who said it reminded her of a choice she made years ago. That's exactly what I wanted—something made from my experience resonating with someone else's. This is why I do this.

Nudge: "What choice do you think she saw in it?"

Response: I'll never know for certain, but I imagine it was a moment when she had to move forward without knowing if the ground would hold. Maybe leaving something—a job, a relationship, a place. The piece is about that threshold between safety and the unknown, and how stepping forward is both terrifying and necessary. If she saw that, then I managed to put something true into the world that found someone else who recognized it. That's the whole point, isn't it? Making something alone that connects to someone you've never met.

Had a team standup today and Ravi brought up that our payment processing pipeline is rejecting a disproportionate number of transactions from certain Southeast Asian corridors. The data was right there in the dashboard. Mostly remittance flows, the kind migrant workers use to send money home. Something about the fraud model flagging certain patterns that are just... normal behavior for people sending small amounts frequently.

I knew what I was looking at. I've sat across the table from guys at the worker center who tell me how hard it is to send money back to their families, the fees they pay, the delays. And here's our system making it worse. But when Ravi raised it, the lead just said we'd revisit the thresholds next quarter and moved on. I could have pushed. I had the context, the stories, the data to make a case. But I just nodded and typed something in the chat about checking the logs.

Part of me says it wasn't the right moment, that I need to build a proper case first. But honestly I think I was just worried about being the guy who derails standup with a soapbox moment. My parents always told me not to stick out, keep your head down and do good work. Maybe that advice served them well in a factory town in Fujian. I'm not sure it serves me well here, sitting on information that could change something.

Nudge: "What would you have said if you'd spoken up?"

Response: Probably something about how the people on the other end of those rejected transactions aren't abstractions. They're the same guys I see on Saturday mornings trying to figure out medical claims. But saying that out loud at work feels like crossing a line I can't uncross.

Went to the worker center this morning. There was a new guy, Suresh, from Tamil Nadu. Fell from scaffolding three weeks ago and his employer hasn't filed the injury report. He showed me the bruises on his back, purple-yellow, still swelling around his ribs. I helped him draft a letter to MOM. My Mandarin-accented English and his Tamil-accented English, both of us stumbling through legalese neither of us was built for.

But we got it done. He shook my hand with both of his and said thank you in a way that made me want to cry in the stairwell afterward. I held it together. Barely.

Nudge: "What was it about his thank you?"

Response: It felt undeserved. All I did was help write a letter. He's the one living through it, sleeping on a bunk bed with broken ribs because he can't afford to stop working.

Normal Monday. Dropped Xiao Yu at school and she asked me why some of her classmates' parents drive them and we take the bus. I told her we like the bus. Which is half true. We can afford a car, we just choose not to, but explaining that to a seven-year-old is its own project.

Work was the usual sprint backlog. Nothing exciting, nothing soul-crushing. Had lunch with a colleague and we talked about the new condo launches. Everyone around me seems to be upgrading something, their flat, their car, their phone. I don't feel the pull the way I used to, but I notice it like background noise you can't quite tune out.
Same day, later. Ordered from Grab because I was too tired to cook. While eating I scrolled past an article about plastic waste in the Java Sea. Closed it, kept eating from the styrofoam container.
Rainy morning. The uncle at the kopi stall asked about my daughter. Drank my coffee and watched the wet road for a while before heading to the MRT.

Team dinner tonight. Quarterly thing, company card. The conversation turned to hiring and someone made a comment about how candidates from certain countries need more "handholding." A few people laughed. I didn't laugh but I also didn't say anything. Just moved the ice around in my glass.

The thing is I know exactly what that comment means. I've been on the receiving end of it, the assumption that because you come from a certain place you're somehow less. And I've watched the guys at the center deal with far worse versions of the same attitude every day. But at the table I just sat there because it was a team dinner and I didn't want to make it awkward. I'm so tired of choosing comfort over what I actually think.

Nudge: "Is it always about comfort, or is something else at play?"

Response: I don't know. Probably both. It's easier to blame it on not wanting confrontation than to admit I'm scared of being seen differently by people I work with every day.

Long week but today felt different. Took Xiao Yu to East Coast Park and she spent an hour picking up shells and bits of plastic from the sand. She didn't separate them at first, just collecting, and then she looked at her bucket and said the plastic ones aren't pretty. I asked what we should do with them and she said throw them away properly. We spent another thirty minutes walking to find recycling and trash bins.

It's a small thing. A kid picking up beach trash. But watching her make that distinction on her own, without being told, reminded me of why Mei Ling and I try to live the way we do. Not because we're going to fix the ocean or the climate or anything so grand, but because the habits matter. The noticing matters.

Came home and cooked dinner together, the three of us. Xiao Yu was in charge of washing vegetables. She takes the job very seriously, inspecting each leaf. Mei Ling and I exchanged a look over her head, the kind of look that says we might be doing okay at this. It's been an exhausting few weeks but tonight I feel settled in a way I haven't for a while.

Nudge: "What made tonight different from the other exhausting days?"

Worker center morning. Helped two new guys file their claims. One of them brought me a packet of murukku his wife made. Sat on the bench outside eating it and feeling grateful for this strange second life I have on Saturdays.

Sprint deadline tomorrow and I'm at the office late. The PM asked me to cut corners on the data validation layer for the remittance feature, the same one Ravi flagged weeks ago. She said we'll circle back to it after launch. I know what "circle back" means. It means never.

I said okay. I could have pushed back, could have said the validation matters because real people's money is on the line and if we ship it broken the ones who suffer are the ones who can least afford it. But the deadline is real and I'm tired and sometimes you just want to go home. Chose what was easy for me. The people affected won't even know my name.

The remittance feature launched last week. Already seeing support tickets about failed transfers. Mostly from corridors serving Bangladesh and Myanmar. I pulled the data quietly and it's what I expected, the validation issues I was asked to skip are causing real failures for real people.

Brought it up in a 1:1 with my lead. He agreed it's a problem but said the next sprint is already planned and we can't squeeze it in. I nodded. Again. I keep telling myself I'll escalate next time, build the case, find the right moment. But the right moment keeps not arriving and meanwhile people can't send money to their families.

Nudge: "How many right moments have passed now?"

Response: Too many. I've lost count. At some point the excuse stops being about timing and starts being about me.

Xiao Yu asked me at dinner what I do at work. I gave the usual answer, daddy helps move money from one place to another so people can buy things. She asked if I help everyone and I said I try to. But sitting there with her looking at me I felt the gap between that answer and reality like a physical weight.

Mei Ling could tell something was off. She squeezed my hand under the table. Later I told her about the remittance tickets and she asked me what I was going to do. I said I didn't know. She said that's a start, at least you're not pretending you're fine with it. She's right. I'm not fine with it. I've been choosing convenience over doing what I know matters and each time it gets a little easier to justify, which is exactly what scares me.

Nudge: "What's one thing you could do this week?"

My sister asked me if I was "doing okay" on the phone last night with that voice she uses when she's actually asking if I'm depressed. I'm not depressed. I made banana bread with Olive yesterday and we painted rocks in the backyard and I reorganized the entire pantry because it was driving me insane. That's not depression, that's Tuesday.

She doesn't get it because she's single and works in marketing and goes to Barcelona on a whim. Good for her. I chose this. I'm not sitting here wishing I had her life. I just wish mine had a few more surprise elements built in. Like a plot twist every now and then. Something I didn't see coming at 6am when I set the cereal bowls out.

Nudge: "What kind of plot twist are you hoping for?"

Response: Honestly? I don't even know. That's the part that scares me. I can't name the thing I want. I just know the absence of it. Like there's a shape missing from my week and I keep filling it with pantry organization and sourdough starters.

Spent three hours at a pumpkin patch watching kids run in circles. I stood there holding juice boxes like a human vending machine. Got home and Kyle asked how my day was and I said "fine" and went to bed at 8:30.

Nudge: "What would've made it better?"

Response: It was fine. Kids had fun. That's the point, right?
Signed up for a ceramics class on Thursday evenings. Kyle said he'd handle bedtime. First class is next week. Already looking forward to getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh.

First ceramics class happened. Small studio off Broadway, eight people, mostly women, a couple retirees, one guy who looked like he was there because of a breakup. The instructor is this woman named Greta who has clay permanently embedded in her fingernails and talks about centering the clay like it's a spiritual practice.

I threw my first cylinder and it collapsed immediately. The second one wobbled but held. Greta said I had "aggressive hands" which I think was a compliment. My nails are ruined and I got clay in my hair and I didn't check my phone once in two hours. Didn't even think about it.

Drove home with the windows down even though it was forty degrees. Played music loud. Walked in the door and the house was quiet, kids asleep, Kyle on the couch watching something about the stock market. He asked if I had fun. I said yes. He said "good" and went back to his show.

I stood in the kitchen eating leftover pasta straight from the container and felt something I haven't felt in a while. Not happiness exactly. More like I'd briefly been someone else. Or maybe someone I used to be.

Nudge: "Someone else, or someone you used to be?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe both. Maybe they're the same thing at this point.

Can't sleep. Kyle is snoring. I'm in the kitchen with the light over the stove and my phone and a glass of wine that I'm pretending is just one glass even though I refilled it.

Looked at flights to Mexico. Not for any reason. Just wanted to see how much it would cost. $340 round trip to Puerto Vallarta. That's less than what I spend on groceries in a week for this family. I could go for a weekend. I won't. But I could.

Scrolled through my old Instagram from 2017, before Olive was born. There's a photo of me at a music festival in Austin with body paint on my arms and a flower crown and I'm laughing so hard my eyes are closed. I don't recognize that person. Or I do, but she feels like a character from a show I watched a long time ago. The plot moved on and she got written out.

The wine is warm now. I should go to bed. Tomorrow is a half day at school for Mason which means I need to figure out lunch and an afternoon activity and probably a trip to Target because he needs new sneakers. Again. Nine-year-old boys destroy shoes like it's a competitive sport.

I just want one day where I don't know what's going to happen next. Where the schedule doesn't exist and I can just follow whatever sounds interesting. Is that so unreasonable?

Nudge: "What would you actually do with that day?"

Response: Drive somewhere I've never been. No map. Stop when something catches my eye. Eat at a restaurant where I can't read the menu. Talk to strangers. Stay out past dark. I've thought about it enough that I have a whole itinerary, which kind of defeats the purpose.

Same night, still up. Kyle got up to use the bathroom and saw me in the kitchen and asked what I was doing. I said "just thinking." He said "about what?" and I said "nothing" and he shrugged and went back to bed. That's the whole conversation. That's what passes for connection at midnight in this house.

I'm not blaming him. He works. He's tired. He handles bedtime when I go to ceramics. He doesn't complain. But he also doesn't ask. Not really. He asks the way you ask a coworker in the elevator. "How was your day?" is not the same as "How are you?" and neither of those is the same as "What do you need?"

Nobody asks me what I need. I'm the person who asks everyone else. What do you want for lunch. Did you brush your teeth. Where's your backpack. Do you need a jacket. I am a logistics coordinator for four other humans and my own needs are somewhere on page twelve of a to-do list that only goes to page three.

I know how this sounds. I know every parent feels this way sometimes. I know I'm not special. I know people have real problems and mine is that I'm bored and restless in a nice house in Littleton, Colorado with a functioning dishwasher and a husband who doesn't yell. I know that. But knowing it doesn't make the feeling go away. It just makes me feel guilty for having it.
