Halloween aftermath. Candy wrappers everywhere. Olive had a meltdown over her princess costume ripping. Mason ate too much sugar and threw up. Lucy was fine because Lucy is always fine. I picked glitter out of the carpet for forty minutes.

Second ceramics class. Made a bowl that actually looks like a bowl. Greta said my centering is improving. Hands are sore. Good sore. The kind of tired that means you did something, not the kind that means the day happened to you.

Kyle and I had takeout Thai after the kids went down. He mentioned his office is doing a retreat to Tahoe in January. No partners. I said "sounds fun" and meant it, but also felt something heavy settle in my stomach.

Nudge: "What was the heavy thing?"

Response: Jealousy, I think. Not of Tahoe. Of having something on the calendar that's just for you. That nobody scheduled for you. That you get to anticipate without coordinating childcare first.

Parent-teacher conference for Mason. Teacher said he's doing well in math, needs work on reading comprehension. Brought cupcakes for Lucy's class birthday. Picked up dry cleaning. Groceries. Normal stuff.

Olive got sick, so I missed ceramics. Sat in her room rubbing her back while she slept and scrolled through the class group chat watching everyone post photos of their glazed pieces from last week. Mine is still sitting on the drying shelf. Greta texted me separately and said she'd fire it for me.

I know missing one class isn't a big deal. But it felt like something being taken away. Like the universe reminding me that I don't actually get to have my own thing. Every time I carve out something that's mine, a fever or a school closure or a dentist appointment swallows it back up. I sat there watching Olive breathe and felt both tender and trapped at the same time.

Nudge: "Can you hold both of those at once?"

My college friend Dana visited this weekend. She lives in Portland, runs a food truck, just got back from a month in Vietnam. She showed up with good wine and stories about motorbike rides through rice paddies and a tattoo she got from a guy in Hanoi. The kids loved her because she's loud and funny and let them paint her toenails.

After they went to bed she asked me the question everyone eventually asks: "Do you miss working?" And I gave my usual answer which is that I don't miss the job, I miss the energy. The feeling of making something happen. The chaos of event day when everything could go wrong and you're the one holding it together with duct tape and charm.

She said I should start planning events again, freelance. Small stuff. Birthday parties, local festivals. I said maybe. She said "you always say maybe" and she's right. I do always say maybe. Maybe is how you say no without having to defend it.

But here's what I didn't tell Dana. I don't want to plan other people's good times anymore. I want to have my own. I want to be the person at the festival, not the person who booked the porta-potties. I want to be surprised by something. I want to walk into a room and not know what's about to happen. That's what I miss. Not the logistics. The feeling.

Kyle wouldn't understand that. He'd say I can go to a festival anytime. He'd buy me tickets and think he'd solved the problem. But it's not about the ticket. It's about the fact that somewhere along the way, I became the kind of person who needs permission to enjoy things, and I don't know how to undo that.

Nudge: "Who are you asking permission from?"

Response: Everyone. No one. Myself, probably. I don't know. It's just easier to say maybe and keep making lunches.

The gallery wants to change the whole color palette for the installation, said it's "too somber for the spring collection." Spring collection. Like my work is a handbag.

Nudge: "What matters most to you about this piece?"

Response: It's about plastic waste in the North Sea. If I make it cheerful and decorative, I'm just adding to the noise. The discomfort is the point.

Spent the weekend at the refugee art workshop. We had twelve participants this time, up from eight last month. One woman, Fatima, has been coming consistently since October. She doesn't speak much Dutch yet, but her drawings are extraordinary - detailed botanical illustrations of plants from her home region in Syria that she does from memory.

I brought some of my old nature journals from when I was her age, thinking we might find common ground. We sat together for most of the afternoon, not really talking, just drawing side by side. At one point she reached over and corrected my shading technique on a leaf. I was the student.

The session ran two hours over. I had planned to work on my installation sketches this evening, but I sat in the car for twenty minutes before driving home, just thinking about those plants that no longer exist where she once lived. The commission deadline feels less urgent somehow, even though I know it shouldn't.

Nudge: "What shifted for you in that car?"

Response: I was thinking about permanence. My gallery piece will be dismantled in three months. Fatima's drawings preserve something real that's been destroyed. I started questioning whether my environmental commentary means anything compared to someone actually documenting loss.

Just got off a call with my sister. She asked when I'm going to "get serious" about retirement planning. I'm 52. I have a studio, commissions, work that matters. But because I don't have a pension and a holiday fund, I'm apparently not a real adult.

She means well. I know she worries. But every conversation becomes a referendum on my life choices. Her husband works in insurance, they have a nice house in Utrecht, the kids are in good schools. And I have paint under my fingernails and a second-hand car. Different doesn't mean wrong.

Later the same day. Maybe the call bothered me more than I admitted. I do think about money sometimes. Not having it, I mean.

Nudge: "What does security actually look like to you?"

Response: Not what my sister has. But maybe enough to not panic when a commission falls through. I've been pretending money doesn't matter because wanting it feels like a betrayal of why I do this.

Long week. The gallery accepted my color choices after I sent them a three-page explanation of the concept. They didn't love it, but they agreed. Small victory. I should feel satisfied but mostly I feel tired.

The nonprofit board meeting ran three hours last night. We're losing our main funder and no one can agree on next steps. Half the board wants to chase corporate sponsors, half thinks that compromises our mission. I sat there thinking about how much simpler it was when I just showed up and ran workshops. Now there's strategy and stakeholders and "capacity building."

Nudge: "What's getting squeezed out by all this?"
Saw the question about what's getting squeezed. I'm not avoiding it. I just don't have a neat answer. My studio time, obviously. Maybe that's fine for now.
Good day in the studio. Lost track of time, which hasn't happened in weeks. The installation is finally taking shape the way I imagined it.

Installation opens next week and I'm behind. Skipped the workshop on Saturday to finish the main panel, which was the right choice for the deadline but felt wrong in a different way. Fatima asked after me, apparently. One of the other volunteers said she seemed disappointed.

I've been telling myself that the artwork has meaning beyond just me, that it might change how someone sees the world. But sitting here at midnight with resin fumes and sore hands, I wonder if I'm just making excuses for choosing my career over the people I said I cared about.

Nudge: "Is this a one-time choice or a pattern?"

Response: Pattern. I know it's a pattern. The teaching job, my marriage, now this. I keep choosing work. I tell myself it's because the work matters, but maybe I just find people harder.
Opening night went well. Several pieces sold. A journalist from NRC wants to interview me about the environmental themes.

Same day, later. The opening was a success by every external measure and I spent the last hour sitting in my studio in the dark.

A collector asked me to explain the piece about North Sea plastic. I gave my usual explanation - the scope of the problem, the research, the materials I used. He nodded, bought the piece, and then asked if I could do something similar but "more hopeful" for his holiday home. I smiled and said I'd think about it.

I should have said no. I should have said art isn't interior decoration. But I need the money, and he wasn't wrong that people want hope. And maybe I'm the one who's wrong, clinging to this idea that discomfort creates change when actually people just look away.

I keep thinking about what I said in the last question - that I choose work because people are harder. But tonight I chose people, in a way. I performed the version of myself that makes sales and connections. And I hate it, but I also felt this relief that it was working, that maybe I don't have to be so uncompromising about everything.

Nudge: "What would uncompromising cost you right now?"

Response: Probably the commission. Maybe more. But also - and I hadn't thought this through until now - it might cost me this idea that my choices are purely principled. Some of my stubbornness is just fear of doing it wrong in a different way.

Went back to the workshop today. Fatima was there. She showed me the drawing she finished while I was away - a complete botanical study of a flower I don't recognize, maybe something that grew near Aleppo. We didn't talk about my absence.

I'm tired in a good way tonight. The kind of tired from doing something rather than just worrying about all the things I'm not doing. I emailed the collector to say I'd think about his commission but couldn't promise "hopeful" - I could only promise honest. He hasn't replied. That's probably fine.

Started doing the math again tonight. If I keep the delivery app running five days a week and pick up at least two handyman jobs on the weekends, I can cover everything with about twelve thousand pesos left over. That leftover is supposed to be for the motorcycle insurance renewal in May. I wrote it on a sticky note and put it on the fridge next to the old one that says "DO NOT touch savings."

Funny thing is, I spent Saturday at the parrilla with Cacho and Pocho like always, and the bill came out heavier than usual because we ordered that tira de asado. I didn't blink. Six hours of good food and good talk, what am I going to do, skip it? Some weeks the numbers work, some weeks they don't. But I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should.

My sister Graciela keeps calling to tell me I should go back to a regular job. She doesn't get it. I'm not going to sit in some warehouse again answering to a guy ten years younger than me who treats people like furniture.

Nudge: "What is it about the idea of going back that feels impossible?"

Response: It's not impossible, I just don't want to. There's a difference. Look, I know what I'm doing. I've been managing fine for five years this way. Graciela worries because she's Graciela. She married an accountant, everything in her life has a line item. Not everyone lives like that.

Bad week. The delivery app lowered the per-trip rate again, no warning, no explanation. Just opened the app Monday morning and the numbers were different. I did the same twelve deliveries I always do on a weekday and came home with almost twenty percent less. Twenty percent. That's not nothing. That's a week of groceries, that's Negrita's vet visit I've been putting off.

The handyman side is slower too because people are tightening up. I had three jobs lined up this week and two of them canceled last minute — one lady said she'd "wait until next month" to fix her bathroom faucet and the other one just stopped responding. So now I'm sitting here Thursday night with the calculator app open, moving numbers around like I'm playing some kind of sad puzzle game, trying to figure out where the motorcycle payment comes from.

The thing that really gets to me is the feeling. It's not just about the money, it's the not knowing. Every month is a guess. Every week I wake up and I don't know what I'm going to make. I used to think that was the trade-off for not having a boss breathing down my neck. Lately I'm not so sure the trade-off is worth it. But what am I supposed to do, crawl back and prove Graciela right? No. I'll figure it out. I always figure it out.

Nudge: "You said you're not sure the trade-off is worth it anymore. What would it look like if you really sat with that thought?"

Got a call from Don Alfredo across the street — he needs his whole kitchen faucet replaced plus some tile work. That's a good-sized job, probably two days of work. Felt like I could breathe again for the first time all week.

Motorcycle started making a noise yesterday. A grinding thing when I brake. I took it to Raul's shop and he looked at it and said the brake pads are gone and the disc is warped, it needs replacing. He quoted me a number that wiped out everything I'd saved from the Alfredo job. Every peso.

I stood there in Raul's shop and felt my stomach just drop. Because it's not optional — the motorcycle is how I make deliveries. Without it I'm on foot or taking the subte, which means fewer trips, which means less money, which means next month's rent gets tight. It's like this chain where one thing breaks and everything behind it starts wobbling. I told Raul to do it, because what choice do I have. But I walked home and I couldn't even eat dinner.

Nudge: "When you walked home, what was going through your head?"

Response: Honestly, I was thinking about my old man. He worked at the same factory for thirty-two years. Hated it, but every month the check came in. He never had to wonder if the floor was going to fall out from under him. I used to think that was a sad way to live. Now I'm not so sure. He always had a little envelope in the dresser drawer with cash for emergencies. I don't have that envelope. I don't have anything between me and the next bad surprise. And that thought — that I'm one broken motorcycle or one bad month away from not being able to pay rent — that sat on my chest the whole walk home.

Couldn't sleep. Opened the app at 1am to see if there were any late-night delivery runs. There were two. I took them both, rode the bicycle I borrowed from my neighbor. Came home at 3am with barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas.

Picked up the motorcycle from Raul this morning. It rides clean now, brakes feel solid. That's something. But I'm running on maybe four hours of sleep from the last few days and I can feel it in my eyes, in the way I'm slow to react at intersections. Almost clipped a bus mirror on Corrientes.

Saturday is coming and Cacho already texted about the parrilla. Part of me wants to skip it this week, save the money. But another part of me — the louder part — says that's the one thing that keeps me sane. You take away the asado with friends and what do I have left? A cat, a calculator, and a leaking faucet in my own bathroom that I haven't even bothered to fix.

Nudge: "When you say the parrilla is the one thing that keeps you sane — what does it give you that nothing else does?"

Response: It's the only time during the week where I'm not counting anything. Not counting deliveries, not counting pesos, not counting hours. I sit down, the meat comes, somebody pours the wine, and for a few hours I'm just Roberto having a good time with his friends. Nobody's asking me to fix something or deliver something or be somewhere in fifteen minutes. It's the closest thing I have to feeling like my life is actually mine.

Don Alfredo recommended me to his daughter for some painting work at her new apartment. Three rooms, good rate, she even offered to pay half upfront. I bought Negrita the fancy wet food she likes to celebrate.

Graciela came over unannounced today while I was between deliveries. She brought empanadas, which was nice, except then she sat at my kitchen table and started doing that thing she does where she looks around the apartment cataloguing everything that's wrong. The peeling paint above the window, the chair with the broken leg, the fact that I still don't have a proper desk and eat on the couch.

Then she said — and I quote — "Roberto, you're almost fifty, you can't live like a college student." And I just stood there holding an empanada feeling like I was fourteen again getting lectured. She doesn't see that I actually like my life most of the time. I like picking my own hours. I like riding through Palermo on a Tuesday morning with the sun out and nobody telling me where to be. But she looks at this apartment and she sees failure. And the worst part is that sometimes, when she leaves, I start seeing it through her eyes too.

Nudge: "You said sometimes you see it through her eyes. What do you see when that happens?"

Response: A guy who's been treading water for five years and calling it swimming. Someone who left a stable situation because of pride and never figured out the next step. I see the broken chair and I think, why haven't I fixed that? I'm literally a handyman. And the answer is because when I come home I don't want to fix anything else, I just want to sit down and not move. And that scares me a little because my old man never left things broken. He always had the house perfect. I can't even keep up with my own apartment and I live alone with a cat.

Painting job at Don Alfredo's daughter's place is done. She was happy with it, paid the balance, even tipped me a little extra. So the money situation is stable for the moment. Rent is covered, motorcycle payment is covered, Raul is paid. There's a small cushion again, not much, but it's there.

Went to the parrilla Saturday like always. Pocho brought his nephew, a kid of about twenty-five who does the same delivery app I do but also drives for a rideshare service on the side. He was telling me about his "strategy" — how he works the surge hours, how he tracks which neighborhoods tip best, how he's saving for a down payment on his own place. The whole table was impressed. I just sat there eating my chorizo and feeling old.

It's not jealousy exactly. The kid has energy and optimism and a plan, and good for him. But I was that kid once. I had the energy to work sixteen-hour days and still go dancing with my ex-wife on Saturday night. Now I do eight hours of deliveries and my back locks up. I eat the asado and drink the wine and it's still good, it's always good. But there's this thin layer underneath the enjoyment now, like a film on old coffee. Like I'm enjoying it because I know this might not last. Like I should be saving what I'm spending here but I can't bring myself to stop because this is the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival.

Nudge: "You described a thin layer underneath the enjoyment. When did you first start noticing it?"

Fixed the chair today. The one Graciela noticed. It took me twenty minutes. I don't know why I left it broken for so long. I also patched the peeling paint above the window. Not because she said anything but because once I started with the chair I just kept going. Negrita watched me from the couch like she was supervising.

After I finished I sat in the chair and it felt solid. Stupid thing to feel good about but I did. I texted Cacho and told him I'd bring a bottle of the good Malbec tomorrow — the Catena, not the box wine we usually get. Sometimes you just want to do something a little extra for no reason. I've got the painting money still in my pocket. Next week is uncertain, it always is. But right now the chair is fixed, the wall looks clean, and tomorrow I'll eat well with people I like. That's not nothing.

Can't sleep. Kept redoing the sketch for the Suba district mural because the committee wants something "uplifting" and every version I draw keeps drifting toward the river contamination. My hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise.

Nudge: "What would happen if you showed them your real sketch?"

Response: They'd smile politely and tell me to try again. That's how these things work — you read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive. It's not about my sketch.

Good day at the workshop. One of the teenagers, David, showed up with a collage about his neighborhood flooding last year — garbage and river water mixed together. He made it beautiful without sanitizing it. I wish I had his nerve sometimes.

Nudge: "What do you think gives him that nerve that you wish you had?"

Response: He doesn't have a committee reviewing his work. He doesn't have a landlord or a mother who calls every Sunday asking if the grants are coming through. David just has cardboard and glue and something he needs to say. There's a freedom in that. I used to have it too, I think, before I learned all the reasons to hold back.

Meeting with Patricia from the environmental collective went better than expected. She's organizing a march next month about the deforestation in Guaviare and wants muralists to paint temporary pieces along the route. No committee, no brief, no polite rewrites — just paint what you see and feel. I said yes before I finished my coffee.

Spent the afternoon looking at satellite images of the cleared areas. It's staggering how fast the green disappears between one photo and the next. I started sketching a piece that layers the satellite view underneath a portrait of a family I met two years ago when I traveled there for a residency. They had this small farm at the edge of the forest. I keep wondering if that farm still exists.

The march is February 28. Patricia asked if I'd also speak at the rally beforehand. I told her I'd think about it. Speaking is different from painting — you can't hide behind brushstrokes. My mother would probably tell me to just do the mural and not draw attention to myself. She still worries about artists getting into trouble. But Patricia's right that the work means more when the person behind it shows up.

The Suba mural committee approved a revised design. I compromised — kept the river but softened the contamination imagery into something more abstract. Flowing blues and greens instead of the murky brown I originally wanted. They loved it. Said it was "inspiring." I nodded and thanked everyone and then sat in my car for ten minutes staring at the steering wheel.

I keep telling myself this is how it works. Public art is negotiation. You serve the community's vision, not just your own. And maybe the abstracted version will still land — maybe someone will look at those blues and sense that something is wrong underneath. That's the hope, anyway. But part of me keeps hearing David's collage in my head, all that directness, no translation required.

Called my cousin Marcela tonight. She's a lawyer in Medellin, very proper, always knows the right thing to say at family dinners. I told her about the march and she got quiet for a long time. Then she said, "Lu, you know how tia Rosa talks. Just be careful what you attach your name to." And I know she means it with love. I know the family worries. But careful has a cost too, and I'm starting to wonder how much it's adding up.

Nudge: "When you say careful has a cost — what does that cost feel like day to day?"

Response: Like a low hum. This background noise of things I chose not to say, images I softened, opinions I kept to dinner with close friends. None of it is dramatic on its own. But when I look at my portfolio from five years ago versus now, the work is technically better but quieter. The edges got filed down somewhere. I'm not sure when it happened exactly.

Family group chat exploded today. My uncle Hernando posted a news article about the Guaviare deforestation — same issue the march is about — and wrote "sad but what can you do, these people need to eat." I started typing a response three times. Deleted all of them.

It's not that I disagree entirely — the economics of it are real and I've seen the poverty firsthand. But the framing drives me insane. "What can you do" as if there are no choices, no policies, no one responsible. I wanted to write about the logging companies, the lack of enforcement, the families who actually live there being displaced. But Hernando is my mother's brother and I can already picture the awkward silence at Easter dinner if I go off in the group chat.

So I sent a sad emoji and closed the app. Mature. Professional. Utterly hollow. Patricia would have posted a five-paragraph essay with citations. I admire that and also cannot imagine being that person in my own family. The mural for the march — that I can do. Words in the chat where my grandmother can see them — that's different somehow, and I hate that it's different.

Finished the base layer of the march mural today. It's honest work. Stood back and felt something I haven't felt in a while — that the paint is saying exactly what I mean.

Nudge: "Sounds like something clicked. What's different about this piece?"

My mother called. Someone told her about the march mural. She didn't yell — she never yells — she just got very quiet and asked "is this going to be a problem for you?" That question does more damage than yelling ever could.

Nudge: "What does her worry do to you?"

Response: Makes me want to reassure her, mostly. I told her it's just a painting, nothing political, which is not entirely true. But she doesn't need to carry that. She has enough.
Long day prepping supplies for Friday. Shoulders ache. Didn't think about the committee or my mother or the march. Just mixed pigments and cleaned brushes. Sometimes that's enough.

Two days until the march. Patricia confirmed I'm on the speaker list. I told her yes three weeks ago and have been quietly hoping she'd forget or the schedule would change. It didn't. So now I'm sitting here at midnight trying to figure out what to say to a crowd when I couldn't even type a paragraph in my family's group chat.

The mural is done. I'm proud of it — really proud. It shows the forest canopy dissolving into ash at the edges, with small figures of families walking along the border between green and grey. It's direct without being preachy, I think. But speaking is a different exposure. My name attached to words, recorded on someone's phone, shared. My uncle could see it. My mother definitely would hear about it. Marcela's voice keeps playing in my head: "be careful what you attach your name to."

I keep going back and forth. One minute I think: this matters more than anyone's comfort, including mine. The next minute I think: who am I to stand on a stage when I can't even push back on a mural committee? Maybe I should just let the painting speak and stay behind it where I'm good. But Patricia asked me specifically because she said artists need to be visible, not just their work. And she's not wrong. I just wish being visible didn't feel like standing in traffic.

Nudge: "If you speak and your family hears — what's the worst that actually happens?"

The march was last Friday. I spoke. It was short — maybe three minutes — and my voice shook the entire time. I talked about how art can hold what policy reports can't, the texture of a place disappearing, the face of someone losing their home to a decision made in an office they'll never enter. Patricia squeezed my arm when I sat down. The crowd was bigger than I expected.

My mother hasn't mentioned it. Marcela sent a one-word text: "brave." I don't know if that's approval or a warning. Hernando liked a photo of the mural on Patricia's Instagram, which is confusing. The sky didn't fall. The family didn't fracture. I feel lighter than I have in weeks, and also a little raw, like a layer of skin is missing. Not sure if I'd do it again. But I'm glad I did it once.

Nudge: "What surprised you most about how it felt?"

Response: That nobody was angry. I'd built this whole catastrophe in my head — my mother crying, my uncle calling me irresponsible, the family closing ranks. None of it happened. The worst outcome was silence, and silence turned out to be bearable. The actual surprise was that the shaking in my voice didn't make me less credible. People came up to me afterward and said it made them listen harder. I don't know what to do with that yet.

Same day, later. The Suba committee emailed asking if I'd be interested in a second mural for a park renovation. They mentioned they saw "my recent public work" and want something with that "raw quality." I read the email four times.

On one hand — this is exactly what I wanted. Someone seeing the unfiltered version and asking for more of it. The kind of work where I don't have to translate myself into something palatable. But then I read the rest of the email and there are guidelines, approval stages, a heritage review board. The same machinery. And I started wondering whether they want the rawness or the idea of rawness, something that looks honest but still fits neatly into a park renovation plan.

I haven't replied yet. Part of me wants to say yes and fight for the real version from inside the process. Part of me wants to say no and keep doing the kind of work where I answer only to the paint. And there's a third part — the loudest one, honestly — that says: just be grateful someone asked, don't be difficult, say thank you and show up and do good work within the lines. That voice sounds a lot like my mother. I don't think she's wrong. I don't think she's entirely right either. Going to sleep on it.

Finally had a day off after six days straight. Spent the whole afternoon at Kite Beach with Nadia and Sara. We found this spot near the food trucks where the sand was perfect, not too hot, and just lay there listening to the waves. Nadia kept trying to get us to go paddleboarding but I told her I just wanted to stay horizontal and feel the sun on my face.

There was this moment when I looked up at the sky and realized I hadn't thought about the hospital in hours. No beeping monitors, no worried parents, no charts to update. Just the sound of the water and my friends laughing about something on their phones. I needed that. I really, really needed that.

Second entry today because we ended up at this new Persian restaurant Nadia found. The tahdig was crispy and perfect and I may have eaten three servings of kashk-e bademjan. My stomach hurts in the best way possible.

Nudge: "What made tonight special beyond the food?"

Response: It was just nice, you know? Good food, good company. Sometimes that's enough.

Back-to-back twelve-hour shifts. A child in the oncology ward took a turn for the worse and I spent most of yesterday holding her mother's hand while the doctors explained options. I got home at 9 PM both nights and barely had energy to shower before collapsing.

My mother called today asking when I'm going to visit Beirut. I told her maybe in November but honestly I don't know if I can face the family gatherings right now. Everyone will ask about marriage, about why I'm "wasting my best years" in Dubai. I just want to rest without having to justify my choices to anyone.

Nudge: "What specifically about the family gatherings feels hard to face?"

Response: The usual stuff. Lebanese aunties being Lebanese aunties. It's fine, I'll deal with it.

Charge nurse Fatima made another comment today about my "lifestyle." We were in the break room and she was talking about how she works double shifts to save for her daughter's education, and then she looked at me and said "some of us have responsibilities." As if I don't work hard. As if nursing isn't exhausting whether you stay for eight hours or sixteen.

I don't understand why taking care of myself is seen as selfishness. I give everything to my patients when I'm on shift. I held that mother's hand for three hours on Tuesday. I stayed late to comfort a seven-year-old who was scared of his IV. But because I don't sacrifice my entire existence to work, I'm somehow less committed?

My father worked himself into a heart attack at fifty-two. I watched him in that hospital bed, gray and diminished, and I swore I would never let a job steal my life from me. Fatima doesn't know that. Nobody at work knows that. They just see a young woman who goes to the beach and assume she doesn't take anything seriously.

Nudge: "What was it like watching your father in that hospital bed?"

Response: Terrifying. He looked so small. He was always the strongest person I knew, the one who fixed everything, and suddenly he couldn't even lift a glass of water. I remember thinking: this is what happens when you give everything to work and keep nothing for yourself. I was nineteen. I had just started nursing school. I promised myself I would never end up like that.

Booked a weekend trip to Muscat with Sara. We found a resort with an infinity pool overlooking the Gulf. I've been counting down the days.

Nudge: "What are you most looking forward to about this trip?"

Back from Muscat. We spent Saturday morning at the fish market in Muttrah, watching fishermen unload their catch while the call to prayer echoed across the harbor. Sara haggled with a vendor for some dried limes and we both got these beautiful silver bangles from an old souk.

The afternoon we did nothing. Absolutely nothing. Lay by the pool with our books, ordered room service, watched the sunset turn the water gold and pink. Sara said she felt guilty for not "doing more" while we were there, but I told her this is doing something. Rest is doing something. Enjoying the moment is doing something. I think she actually believed me by Sunday.

Nudge: "Why was it important to you that Sara believe that?"

Response: Because I see myself in her. The guilt, the feeling like you should always be productive, always achieving something. I used to feel that way too before I decided to stop. If I can help her let go of that even a little, maybe she won't burn out the way I almost did.

My cousin Rania got engaged. The family WhatsApp group exploded with congratulations and my mother immediately called to tell me, her voice dripping with that particular tone she uses when she's comparing me to someone else. "Rania is younger than you, habibi. Just something to think about."

I am thinking about it. I'm thinking that Rania is marrying someone her parents introduced her to after three months of knowing him. I'm thinking that she quit her job because he wants her to focus on "starting a family." I'm thinking that none of that sounds like the life I want.

But I didn't say any of that. I just said congratulations and asked about the wedding date. Because what's the point of explaining? They don't understand that some of us want different things. That not wanting what they want doesn't mean I'm broken or confused or going through a phase.

There's a new resident at the hospital, Dr. Faris. Young, intense, the type who reads medical journals in the break room. We got to talking today during a slow hour and he asked me what I do for fun. When I mentioned the beach and restaurants and travel, he looked at me like I was speaking a foreign language.

He said he hasn't taken a vacation in three years. Three years. He seemed almost proud of it, like suffering is a badge of honor. I wanted to ask him what the point of becoming a doctor was if he's never going to enjoy being alive, but I just nodded and changed the subject.

It made me think about how different people measure their lives. Some people count achievements, promotions, publications. I count good meals, beautiful sunsets, moments of genuine laughter with people I love. Neither is wrong, I guess. But I know which one I want on my deathbed.

Nudge: "What made you so certain about what you want on your deathbed?"

Response: I don't know if I'm certain. It's just how I try to live. Thinking too much about dying feels morbid.

Lost a patient today. Eight years old. Leukemia. I've been doing this for five years and it never gets easier. You tell yourself not to get attached but how can you not when you're adjusting her pillows and bringing her extra apple juice and watching her try to be brave for her parents?

I went straight to the beach after my shift. Sat there in my scrubs watching the waves until the sun went down. I don't know what else to do with grief except let it sit next to me for a while. The water doesn't ask anything of you. It just keeps moving.

Nudge: "What do you think she would have wanted for you after today?"

Response: I don't know. Probably to go home and eat something. She was always asking if I ate lunch.

Nadia's birthday dinner last night. We went to this rooftop place in JBR with the Dubai Marina glittering below us. Thirteen of us around a long table, sharing mezze and laughing so loud the tables around us kept looking over. I don't even remember what we were laughing about. Just the feeling of being surrounded by people who like being alive.

After dinner we walked along the marina and Nadia said this was the happiest she'd been in months. Work has been crushing her and she almost canceled tonight because she felt guilty taking time for herself. I told her what I always tell people: the work will always be there. The moments won't. I don't know if I'm wise or just stubborn, but I believe it.

Nudge: "Where did you learn to see it that way?"

Response: From watching my father after his heart attack. He said the worst part wasn't almost dying. It was realizing how much of his life he'd missed while he was busy working. He told me to never make the same mistake. I listened.
Long week. Flu season starting early. I'm off for the next three days and I have zero plans except sleeping in and maybe getting a massage. That's the whole plan. That's enough.

Went home to Beirut for a long weekend. Faced the aunties. Survived. Actually more than survived.

Something shifted this time. When my aunt Huda started the usual questions about when I'm settling down, I just smiled and said I'm very happy with my life as it is. She looked confused, like she didn't have a script for someone who isn't apologizing or getting defensive. My mother was watching and I could tell she wanted to jump in and explain me, but she didn't. She just let me be.

Later my father pulled me aside on the balcony while everyone was eating. He said he's proud of me. That I figured out something at thirty that took him fifty years and a heart attack to understand. That life is for living, not for impressing people who won't remember you anyway. Coming from him, that meant everything.

I flew back to Dubai this morning and went straight to the beach. Watched the sunset alone this time. Felt full in a way I can't quite describe. Not because anything big happened, but because I'm finally living the life I actually want, and the people who matter most are starting to see it too.

Another Monday spent optimizing database queries for a billing system. The work pays well, certainly better than anything I could have imagined when I left the village. But today I walked past the construction site where migrant workers are building our new office tower, and I saw a child no older than ten carrying bricks. Her mother was mixing cement nearby.

I wanted to say something, to do something. Instead I walked to my air-conditioned desk and wrote code that helps rich hospitals bill patients more efficiently. The disconnect between my daily labor and what I believe matters is growing wider every year.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying something?"

Couldn't sleep last night. Kept seeing that girl's face. Called my mother in the morning and she reminded me that I too carried water from the well before school when I was her age.

Nudge: "What did her saying that bring up for you?"

Response: It made me feel ashamed in a different way. Like I've become so removed from where I came from that I've forgotten what survival looks like. My mother wasn't excusing the child labor, she was reminding me that I've had the privilege to forget.
Sent the monthly transfer to the village school. Also enrolled for an online course on building accessible software for low-resource settings. Start next week.

The course is fascinating. We are learning about designing for users with limited internet, older phones, and varying literacy levels. It reminded me of my grandmother who never learned to read but could recognize patterns in kolam designs that would challenge any algorithm I could write.

I mentioned the course to Raghav at work today. He laughed and asked why I was bothering with "social service" projects when I could be learning AI tools that would increase my market value. I didn't have a good answer in the moment, but walking home I realized: the question itself reveals different assumptions about what work is for.

For Raghav, work is a transaction, skills exchanged for money. For me, at this stage in life, it feels like there should be more. Not everyone has thirty years of experience building systems. Maybe that expertise could serve people who actually need it, not just corporations looking to squeeze more margins. But then again, my daughter's tuition fees come from those same corporate margins. The math doesn't always work out cleanly.

Nudge: "Has Raghav always seen things that way?"

Response: He's younger, maybe mid-thirties. Grew up in a different India than I did, one where software engineers are heroes and startups are the new temples. I don't blame him. When I was his age, I was chasing the same things. The difference is I've reached them now and I'm asking what comes after.

Lakshmi suggested I should consider early retirement and volunteer full-time with some NGO. Easy for her to say when she has her own pension secured. I know she means well, but she doesn't understand how much of my identity is tied to being a working professional.

Also, and I didn't say this to her, there's fear. What if I give up the corporate world and realize I'm not actually useful to anyone? At least here, however meaningless the work feels, I have metrics proving my value. Pull requests merged, systems delivered, performance reviews completed.

Nudge: "What would 'useful' look like outside of work metrics?"

Response: I don't know. That's the honest answer. I've been measured by numbers for so long that I can't imagine what value looks like without them. Maybe helping someone learn to code who couldn't afford classes? But how would I know if I was actually making a difference?

Sprint planning ran until 7 PM. We're building features no one asked for because some VP read an article about what competitors are doing. I sat there for four hours nodding along, contributing where required, while part of me was calculating how many village school teachers' salaries could be paid with the cost of this one unnecessary feature.

I know this kind of thinking isn't productive. You can't compare everything to some moral baseline or you'll go mad. But today I'm too tired to pretend the comparison doesn't exist in my head.

Nudge: "Why does that comparison keep coming back?"

Response: Because I remember what it felt like to have nothing and know that luck was the only difference between me and so many others. Every rupee spent on nonsense feels like stolen opportunity for someone who could have used it better. I know that's not how economics works, but the feeling doesn't care about economics.

Video call with Priya tonight. She's doing well in Toronto, enjoying her research on climate modeling. She asked about my work and I found myself describing it with more cynicism than usual. She paused and then said something that stuck: "Appa, you taught me that every small action matters. Why don't you believe that for yourself?"

She's right, of course. I've always told her and her generation that individual choices ripple outward, that systemic change starts with personal conviction. But somewhere along the way I started feeling like my own choices were too small to matter against the scale of suffering I see.

The conversation reminded me of why I became an engineer in the first place. Growing up, technology represented possibility, a way out of predetermined fates. I wanted to build things that would make life better. When did I lose that thread? Or maybe I haven't lost it, I've just buried it under spreadsheets and sprint cycles.

Nudge: "What would it look like to un-bury it?"

Same day, later. After the call I went for a walk and found myself outside the construction site again. This time the workers had finished for the night. Empty scaffolding and silence. Felt strangely peaceful.

Spoke with HR about reducing to four days a week. They said they would need to discuss with leadership but it might be possible with adjusted compensation. The money difference isn't trivial, but Lakshmi and I talked it through and we can manage.

The fifth day I want to dedicate to teaching coding at a community center in Koramangala that serves domestic workers and their children. I reached out to them last week and they welcomed the offer. First session scheduled for next Friday.

First teaching session did not go as planned. I had prepared carefully, slides on basic programming concepts, examples I thought would be relatable. But the students struggled with the mouse and keyboard basics I had taken for granted. One woman, Kamala, works as a housekeeper in my neighborhood. She spent fifteen minutes trying to type her name because she had never used a computer before.

I felt useless, like I had parachuted in with my fancy knowledge completely missing what they actually needed. By the end I had abandoned my lesson plan entirely and we spent the last hour just practicing how to open and close windows on the screen.

Walking home I felt deflated, then embarrassed for feeling deflated. Who did I think I was, expecting to transform lives in one session? This is going to be harder and slower than I imagined. The question is whether I have the patience and humility to stick with it.

Nudge: "What would patience look like here?"

Response: I don't know, showing up again next week I suppose. Starting where they are instead of where I expected them to be.

Kamala waved at me this morning. She was walking to work as I was leaving for office. She smiled and said, "Sir, I practiced. I can type my name now." Such a small thing, and yet.

Maybe this is what it looks like. Not grand gestures or systemic overhauls, but one person learning to type her own name for the first time at forty-five. My daughter was right. Every small action matters. The scale of the world's problems paralyzes me when I look at it whole, but zooming into this one interaction, this one victory, there's something real there. Something I helped create.

Nudge: "What changed between yesterday and today?"

Response: Nothing really changed. I just saw her smile and remembered why I wanted to do this in the first place.
Long day. Lost the Hendricks account because their new facilities guy wants to go with a national chain. Grandpa built that relationship in '89 and I just watched it walk out the door.

Dad brought up the Hendricks thing at Sunday dinner. In front of everyone. Said maybe I should have driven out there in person instead of sending a proposal over email. Like I haven't been running seventy hours a week already.

Nudge: "What stung most about your dad's comment?"

Response: That he might be right. Grandpa would've driven out there.

Closed the Meyer Medical Group contract today — biggest commercial deal we've landed this quarter. Stayed late to call Grandpa and tell him before the rest of the family heard. He said "that's my boy" and I don't think I've smiled like that in a month.

Had a call with the regional HVAC association about their young leaders program. They want me to apply for the board. On paper it's a great opportunity — good network, visibility in the industry, looks sharp on a resume. But the meetings are every other Thursday evening, which is when I do my site walkthroughs with the install crews.

Those walkthroughs aren't required. Nobody would notice if I stopped. But Grandpa drilled it into me when I started: you show up where the work happens, you shake the hands of the guys turning the wrenches. It's how you earn trust. Feels wrong to swap that out for a conference call with people trading business cards. But then I look at some of the other managers my age who are on three boards and speaking at conferences while I'm in a crawlspace checking ductwork.

Nudge: "Which one would keep you up at night?"

Response: The walkthroughs. No question. I'd never forgive myself otherwise.

Thinking more about the board thing from yesterday. Turned it down this morning. Polite email, said maybe next year. But now I'm sitting with this feeling like I just closed a door I shouldn't have. My buddy Jake from college is VP of Operations at a company half our size. He's thirty-two. He's on two industry boards, he did that leadership program at Northwestern, and every time I see his LinkedIn I feel this pull in my chest like I'm falling behind some invisible race.

I know comparison is poison. I know that. But it's hard when the scoreboard is right there on your phone. Jake doesn't have a grandfather who spent fifty years building something with his name on it. Jake doesn't sit at a dinner table every Sunday hearing about how things were done the right way. Jake just has a title and a trajectory and nobody asking him to preserve anything.

Meanwhile I'm trying to grow this company AND keep it feeling like the company it's always been. Like those two things are the same direction. Most days they are. But days like today they feel like opposite ends of a rope and I'm standing in the middle.

Nudge: "Which parts of 'the right way' do you actually believe?"

Just realized I wrote twice in one day. Whatever. Came home and Jake posted about keynoting some supply chain conference. Good for him. I'm going to go prep my Q2 numbers because at least those are mine.

Nudge: "What do your Q2 numbers prove that Jake's keynote doesn't?"

Response: That I actually built something real. Forty-three paychecks.

Grandpa's 80th birthday party Saturday. The whole family came — uncles, cousins, people I haven't seen since I was a kid. Mom put together a slideshow with photos from when he first started the company. There's one of him standing in front of his first van, WHITFIELD HEATING hand-painted on the side. He was twenty-six in that picture. Three years younger than I am now.

Uncle Bill gave a toast about how the company was built on handshakes and showing up. Everyone raised their glasses. I watched Grandpa's face and he was trying not to cry. He looked at me across the table at one point and nodded, this small nod like he was passing something forward. I can't explain why that hit so hard.

Later that night I went out to the driveway to get some air and my cousin Travis followed me out. He works in tech in Seattle, does well for himself. He asked me if I ever thought about doing something else, something that was just mine. I told him the company IS mine. He kind of shrugged and said "you know what I mean." I do know what he means. But I also think he doesn't understand what it feels like to be part of something that started before you were born and will keep going after you're gone. There's weight to that. Some days it's an anchor. Other days it's the only thing keeping me steady.

Performance reviews this week. Had to sit down with each of my crew leads and go through their numbers. Most of the conversations went fine but Danny — he's been with us nineteen years, started under Grandpa — pushed back when I talked about the new scheduling software. Said it was "a solution looking for a problem" and that the whiteboard system works fine.

I wanted to snap at him. The whiteboard system loses us hours every week in double-bookings and missed follow-ups. I have the data. But instead I heard myself saying "let's give it a trial run and see how it goes, no pressure." Which is exactly what Grandpa would have done — never force it, bring people along. But part of me thinks Grandpa could afford to be patient because he had decades. I don't feel like I have decades. The market is moving and if we don't modernize the ops side we'll be the company that did great work in the '90s and couldn't keep up.

Nudge: "What would it cost you to overrule Danny and just implement it?"

Response: His respect. And every guy on the floor who remembers Grandpa.

Danny used the new scheduling software today. Without being asked. Came in, saw the tablet on the wall, and just started putting jobs in. Didn't say a word about it. I didn't either.

Nudge: "You didn't say a word — what were you holding back?"

Response: Relief. Like I finally got permission to move forward.

Sunday dinner. Grandpa wasn't feeling well so it was just Mom, Dad, and me. Without Grandpa at the table the whole energy was different. Dad talked about maybe expanding into the residential solar market. I've been thinking about it too — the margins are there, the demand is growing, it's a natural fit. But when I brought it up to Grandpa last month he said "we're heating and cooling people, that's what we do."

I didn't push it. How do you push it with an eighty-year-old man who built the thing from nothing? He earned the right to say no. But tonight Dad said something that's been rattling around in my head since — he said "your grandfather doesn't run the company anymore, Ryan. You do." And I know that's technically true. My name is on the decisions. But it doesn't feel true. It feels like I'm managing a museum and every choice I make is either a restoration or a renovation and I can never tell which one the family wants.

I keep going back to that nod at his birthday party. What was he passing to me exactly? The company as it is? Or the company as whatever I can make it? I genuinely don't know. And I'm scared to ask because what if the answer is something I can't live with.

Nudge: "What would change if he said 'make it yours'?"

Fourth of July at the lake house. The whole family thing — burgers, fireworks, Uncle Bill doing his annual speech about "what this country was built on." I usually tune it out but this year he looked at me during the part about "carrying the torch" and I realized he wasn't just talking about America.

Cousin Travis was there again. This time he asked how the company was doing and I gave him the numbers — up fourteen percent year over year, best Q2 since 2019, retention above ninety percent. He nodded and said "so it's doing well?" and I said yes. Then he asked "but are YOU doing well?" and I didn't have an answer. I said something about being busy, which is true but not what he was asking. The honest answer is I don't separate the two. The company does well, I'm doing well. The company struggles, I struggle. I don't know if that's dedication or if it's a problem.

Nudge: "If the company disappeared tomorrow, what would be left of you?"

Response: I honestly don't know. That's what scares me.

Had a weird moment today. Was driving back from a site visit and passed the old Whitfield Heating building — the original one on Division Avenue that Grandpa worked out of before we moved to the new place in '02. It's a laundromat now. The sign is gone, the awning's different, there's nothing left of what it was. Just a building someone else uses for something else. I pulled over and sat there for a minute. Not because I was sad exactly. More like I was looking at the future.

Someday the new building will be something else too. Someday the name on the trucks will change, or the trucks will be gone, or the whole industry will be different. Nothing I do is permanent. Nothing Grandpa did was permanent. The only thing that lasts is whether you did it right while you were doing it.

Something shifted sitting in that parking lot. Like maybe I've been so focused on holding everything together exactly as it is that I forgot the point was never the building or the sign. It was how you treated people, how you showed up, whether you left things better than you found them. Grandpa didn't preserve his father's business — he built his own. Maybe that's what the nod meant. Not "keep this the same." Just "do it right." I'm still not sure about solar. But I'm less afraid of the question now.

Nudge: "What changed between last month and this parking lot moment?"

Response: I stopped seeing it as either/or. It was never about furnaces.

Look, I know some teachers think the community garden visits are a waste of instructional time. We spent two hours there today and yes, that's time away from test prep. But watching Marcus Jr. (not named after me, just a coincidence) actually engage with something for the first time all year? Seeing him carefully transplant seedlings and ask questions about composting? That matters.

Mrs. Patterson made a comment in the break room about my "field trip habit" but I'm not going to apologize for it. These kids need to understand they're part of something bigger than standardized tests. They need to touch the dirt, see the earthworms, understand where food actually comes from. That's education too.

Didn't call Grandma back again. She left another voicemail about the restaurant anniversary dinner planning and I just... let it go to voicemail. I'll call her tomorrow.

Nudge: "What made you hesitate to pick up?"

Response: Honestly, I know she's going to ask me to give a speech at the 60th anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. My Cantonese is terrible and I keep meaning to practice but I never do. It feels like I'm letting her down every time we talk and she hears how rusty I've gotten.

Principal wants me to use the new standardized curriculum materials for social studies. They come from the district. They're fine, technically. Cover all the state requirements.

But they're so sanitized. The unit on immigration literally has two paragraphs on the Chinese Exclusion Act. Two paragraphs. My great-grandfather couldn't enter this country for decades because of that law. I told the principal I'd supplement with additional materials. She said to be careful about "going off curriculum." Said it in that way that means don't.

Nudge: "So what will you do with those materials?"

Response: I'm going to use the curriculum. But I'm also going to bring in my family photos and tell the real story during the personal narrative unit. They can't tell me I can't share my own family history. At least I don't think they can.

The kids blew my mind today. We were talking about the water cycle and Jordan raised her hand and asked if the water we drink is the same water dinosaurs drank and I said yes, basically, and then she asked if that meant we were connected to dinosaurs and I said yes again and she got this look on her face like something clicked.

Then Carlos said if we're connected to dinosaurs through water we must be connected to each other too and to the fish and to the trees and I just stood there for a second because yes, exactly, that's exactly it. These ten-year-olds just figured out in five minutes what some adults never understand. We spent the rest of the period talking about interconnection and nobody complained and nobody looked at the clock.

Nudge: "What did that moment mean to you?"

Response: It reminded me why I do this job. Not for the test scores or the evaluations. For moments like that where kids discover something true about the world. That we're all connected, that what we do matters beyond ourselves. That's what I want them to carry with them.

Second entry today because I just need to get this out. Went to the grocery store after school, totally normal trip, and I just stood in the produce section for way too long looking at the plastic clamshells of strawberries. They're from Chile. In January. Wrapped in plastic that's going to exist for five hundred years so I can have strawberries in the middle of winter.

I bought them anyway. I bought the stupid strawberries because I was tired and I wanted something sweet and the farmer's market doesn't open until Saturday. And I know one person buying or not buying strawberries doesn't change the global supply chain but I still felt like a hypocrite putting them in my cart.

This is what I mean when I say it's exhausting to care about everything all the time. My students look at me like I have answers but I'm just as stuck as everyone else. I teach them about sustainability and then I buy Chilean strawberries in plastic. I tell them every choice matters and then I make choices that don't align with that. I don't know how to live in this world without constantly feeling like I'm failing at something.

Called Grandma. We talked for forty minutes. I'm going to give the speech at the anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. She's going to help me practice.

Nudge: "How do you feel about that decision?"

Response: Nervous but good. It's important to her. The restaurant is important to the whole family, to the community really. Dad worked there every summer through high school. So did I. People need to hear what it means to us, in the language it's always been spoken in.

The diversity assembly actually worked! I've been pushing for this for two years and today we had speakers from five different community organizations talking to the whole school. Mrs. Okonkwo from the Nigerian cultural center, Mr. Patel from the Hindu temple, someone from the tribal council, and two others.

The kids were so engaged. They asked real questions. One of my students asked Mrs. Okonkwo what she missed most about Nigeria and she started crying a little and then laughed and said "the food, always the food" and everyone laughed and it was this beautiful human moment. This is what school should feel like. Not test prep and discipline charts. Actual humans connecting with each other, learning that the world is bigger than their own street.

Nudge: "What made you push so hard for this?"

Response: When I was a kid there was nobody who looked like me in the history books or the assemblies. I remember how alone that felt. I don't want any of my students to feel that way. I want them to see themselves in school, and I want them to see each other too.

Made dumplings with Mom today for Lunar New Year prep. My hands remember the folding even when my brain forgets the right way to pleat them. She corrected me three times on the same fold and I wanted to be annoyed but honestly I was just grateful she's still here to correct me.

She told me stories about the restaurant again, the early days when Grandpa worked eighteen hour shifts and Grandma handled the front while raising Dad. Stories I've heard a hundred times but they never get old. There's something about flour on your hands and family around you that makes everything feel solid. Like no matter what else is happening in the world, this is real. This is what matters.

Parent-teacher conferences all day. Twelve families. My voice is gone but one mom thanked me for showing her son that being Chinese-American is something to be proud of. That one sentence made the whole exhausting day worth it.

Nudge: "What did it mean to hear that?"

Response: More than I can explain. I spent so many years feeling caught between two worlds, not Chinese enough, not American enough. If I can help even one kid skip that confusion, help them feel whole from the start... that's why I became a teacher. That's the whole point.

Long week. Just long. A lot of grading, a lot of meetings, a lot of everything. The community garden got a small grant from the city though, so that's something. We can finally fix the broken irrigation system and maybe add a few more raised beds for the school partnership program.

Practiced my speech with Grandma over video call. My Cantonese is getting better but still rough. She says the important thing is not perfect pronunciation but speaking from the heart. I'm trying to believe her. The anniversary dinner is in two weeks and I keep rewriting what I want to say.

Nudge: "What do you keep changing in the speech?"

Response: I keep trying to capture what the restaurant means. Not just to our family but to the town. It's where people celebrated graduations and mourned losses. Where the same families have come for sixty years. It's history. It's proof we belong here. I don't know how to say all that in five minutes, in a language I barely speak, without crying.

I know I should have stayed for the whole faculty meeting but I had to leave early for the speech practice. Principal noticed. She didn't say anything but I saw her write something down. I'll deal with whatever comes from that.

Some things are more important than faculty meetings about parking lot protocols. The anniversary dinner is this Saturday and my grandmother is counting on me. The whole family is counting on me. And maybe that sounds dramatic but the restaurant isn't just a business, it's proof that we made it here, that we stayed, that we built something. I'm not going to let a faculty meeting come between me and honoring that. I'm just not.

Got back from Nashik at ten, meeting ran five hours longer than planned. Supplier wants to renegotiate the whole contract because of raw material costs going up again. My back is aching from the drive but the deal isn't done so I'll be back there Wednesday. At least the evening puja helped settle my mind before the drive home.

Nudge: "What about the Nashik deal has you going back so soon?"

Response: It's just business. These things take time. Anyway, the real issue is that my production manager called in sick today and we've got a client audit on Friday.

Aarav called from Singapore this morning, first time in two weeks. He sounded good, talking about some new project at work, some kind of payment infrastructure thing. I don't understand half of what he describes but I can hear when he's fired up about it. He asked about Maa and I told him she's fine, that she made his favorite kaju katli last week and kept a box aside even though nobody's coming to collect it. He laughed but I don't think he caught what I was saying.

It's strange because I built this company partly so there would be something for the next generation to step into. My father had the first small shop, I turned it into a proper operation, and the idea was always that Aarav would take it further. He knows the business, he grew up in it. But he looked at it and chose something else. I respect that, I do. But there's a gap now in how I think about what comes next.

Had a good meeting with the sustainability consultants after lunch. They think we can get the biodegradable line certified by Q3 if we commit the capital. Numbers look solid. I told them I'd decide by end of month. Thirty years ago I would have said yes on the spot but now I sit with things longer. Not sure if that's wisdom or just fatigue.

Nudge: "Is the gap about the company or something bigger?"

Second entry today because I can't sleep and writing seems better than staring at the ceiling fan. Went back to Maa's for Thursday dinner even though it's only Wednesday. She didn't ask why, just set an extra plate. She made dal the way Baba used to like it, with the whole red chilies left in. Sitting at that table, same steel plates we've used since I was a boy, I felt like things made sense for a moment. The dal tasted exactly right.

Came back and sat with the Q1 numbers. Revenue is up eleven percent but margins are thinner because of the raw material situation. The Nashik supplier is squeezing us and he knows we don't have many alternatives for that grade of recycled fiber. I've been in business long enough to know when someone has leverage and is using it. Need to find a second source, but that takes time and relationship-building, and right now I'm stretched.

I keep coming back to what Aarav said on the phone, that he's building something from scratch. I did that too, once. Took my father's small operation and built it into something real. Maybe that's what he needs to do on his own terms. I just wish his terms didn't put him seven thousand kilometers away. Anyway. The dal was good tonight. That's enough for one day.

Rajiv Kulkarni just got written up in Business Standard. They did a full profile, his logistics company apparently crossed the five-hundred-crore mark. We went to COEP together, started our companies the same year. He's always been three steps ahead, or at least that's how it reads in the papers. My company does good work, honest work, but nobody's writing profiles about steady growth in packaging materials. I know comparison is pointless but I looked at that article for too long this morning.

Then I caught myself wondering if Aarav would have stayed if the company was bigger. If there was something more impressive for him to inherit. That's an ugly thought and I know it, but it sat there in my chest for a while before I could push it aside. The truth is the company is solid. We employ over two hundred people, we pay on time, we've never missed a delivery commitment in six years. That means something. It just doesn't make the business papers.

Had the Nashik meeting. Drove back and went straight to the factory to check on the audit prep. Suresh had it mostly handled, which was a relief. Got home past nine. Shobha had kept dinner but I could tell she was irritated I didn't call. She's right. I should have called. I just get tunnel vision when I'm in it.

Nudge: "You called that thought ugly. Why did it stick?"

Response: Because it means I'm measuring my own son's loyalty against a revenue number. And that's exactly the kind of thinking I always told myself I wouldn't fall into. My father never once talked about the size of his shop. He talked about the quality of his paper, the customers who came back for twenty years. Somewhere I started keeping score differently and I don't like what that says.

Shobha brought up the Varanasi trip again at breakfast. Her mother's eightieth birthday is in April and the whole family is expected. She's already booked the train tickets. I told her I'll come for the weekend portion but can't take the full week off. She went quiet in that way she does when she's decided I'm wrong but isn't going to argue about it in the morning.

The thing is, I want to go. I actually do. Shobha's mother has always been kind to me. The ghats in the morning, the aarti at Dashashwamedh — there's a stillness in Varanasi that I don't find anywhere else. And the whole family gathers, cousins I haven't seen in years, the children running around. But the biodegradable line decision needs to happen and I can't hand that off. It's a forty-lakh capital commitment and some things you sign yourself.

Called the Nashik supplier after lunch. Offered a two-year lock-in at current rates. He said he'd think about it. Also interviewed a candidate for the assistant production manager role. Decent on paper but too eager to impress, kept dropping the names of companies he's worked with instead of talking about what he actually did. I'll see two more candidates next week.

Nudge: "When's the last time you sat in that stillness?"

Shobha told her sister I'm only coming for the weekend and apparently the whole family knows now. Got a call from her brother asking if everything is okay with us. Everything is fine. I'm running a company. People act like taking time off is some kind of moral test. My own father worked six days a week until he was seventy-two and nobody questioned his commitment to the family. He showed up when it mattered and so do I.

Signed off on the biodegradable line investment today. Forty-two lakhs when you include the testing certification. It's the right call. Suresh and I went through the projections twice. If the pilot customers convert, we'll break even by Q1 next year. Felt good to make the decision. Some days the work is the clearest thing I have.

Maa asked me tonight if I'm happy. Just like that, over chai, no context. I said of course, what kind of question is that. She just looked at me and sipped her tea. Mothers see through everything. I changed the subject to the new dal recipe she's trying but it sat with me the whole drive home.

Nudge: "What do you think she was seeing?"

Production line for the new biodegradable material started trial runs today. Watched the first batch come through and it's not right yet — the tensile strength is off, tears too easily. The engineers say it's a calibration issue, two or three more iterations. I've heard that before. But the raw material itself feels promising. Held a sheet of it and it had good weight, good texture. These are the moments I still like about this work, when something you decided on paper becomes real in your hands.

Aarav sent photos of his apartment. It's small but he's furnished it well. He has one of Maa's old brass diyas on his bookshelf, the one she gave him when he left. I zoomed in on the photo just to make sure I was seeing it right. He didn't mention it in the message, just the new coffee table and the view. But he kept it. He put it where he could see it.

Had lunch with Patil from the industry association. He's retiring next year and asked if I'd consider taking his seat on the board. I said I'd think about it. Ten years ago I would have jumped at the visibility. Now I'm not sure I want another obligation. The days are already full. But Patil's father and my father knew each other, and there's something about continuing those connections that matters.

Varanasi trip logistics settled. I'll arrive Friday evening instead of Saturday morning. Shobha seemed pleased when I told her, though she didn't make a big deal of it. I moved two meetings and delegated the supplier follow-up to Suresh. The company will survive three days without me. Writing that down feels like something I need to remind myself of more often.

Biodegradable line second trial was better. Tensile strength improved after the temperature adjustment. Still not where we need it for the food packaging spec but trending the right direction. Told the team I want a third trial by next Wednesday with the modified polymer blend. Set a target of reaching spec by mid-April. If we hit it, we can go to the pilot customers with samples before monsoon season complicates the logistics.

Also called Aarav in the evening. Didn't have a specific reason, just wanted to hear his voice. We talked for twenty minutes about nothing in particular, his weekend plans, a temple he found near his flat that does a small morning puja. He said he's been going on Sundays. I didn't know that. I said nothing about it because I didn't want to make it into a thing, but after we hung up I sat on the balcony for a long time feeling something I can't quite name. Relief, maybe. Or recognition.

Nudge: "Relief or recognition — which one sits closer?"

Response: Recognition. Like seeing something of yourself show up in your child without you having to put it there. I never told him to go to temple. He saw me and Maa do it his whole life and now he's finding his own version of it, seven thousand kilometers away. That means something got through. Not the company, not the career path, but something underneath all of that.

Back from Varanasi. The trip was good. Shobha's mother was genuinely surprised we came a day early. We did the aarti on Saturday evening, the whole family standing along the ghat with the flames reflected on the water. My brother-in-law and I talked business for a bit but mostly we just ate too much and let the kids run circles around us. Slept better those three nights than I have in months.

Got to the factory this morning and Suresh had handled everything cleanly. One minor quality flag on a shipment that he resolved. The Nashik supplier accepted the two-year lock-in. Good. Checked the third trial results on the biodegradable line — we're within eight percent of target spec. Close. I told the team to push for one more round by Friday.

Nudge: "You slept better in Varanasi. What was different?"

Response: No alarm. No production numbers to check before anyone else gets in. I woke up to temple bells and Shobha already dressed and ready for the morning walk along the river. I think I've forgotten what it feels like to not be the first person solving problems each day. In Varanasi I was just someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle. There was nothing to prove to anyone. I didn't realize how tired I was of being the one who holds everything until I put it down for three days.

Shobha asked if maybe we could go to Varanasi more often, maybe once a quarter. I said that's not realistic with the business right now, especially with the new product line launching. She said she's not asking me to shut the factory down, just to take a few days. I know she's not being unreasonable. But there's a part of me that tightens up when someone asks me to step away from the work. Like if I stop, everything stops. I know that's not true. Suresh just proved that last week.

Patil called again about the board seat. I told him I'd accept. It felt like the right thing. His father and mine started in the same market forty years ago, and if I can help the next group of small manufacturers the way the association helped me when I was starting out, that's worth the time. Shobha raised an eyebrow when I told her I'd taken on something new the same week she asked for more time off together. She has a point. I'm going to have to sort that out.

Fourth trial on the biodegradable line hit target spec. Finally. The team earned a day off and I told them so. Sent Aarav a photo of the sample sheet. He wrote back, "Looks like the future." Three words, but they landed right.
